title: Slang Context-based Inference Enhancement via Greedy Search-Guided Chain-of-Thought Prompting
summary: Slang interpretation has been a challenging downstream task for Large Language Models (LLMs) as the expressions are inherently embedded in contextual, cultural, and linguistic frameworks. In the absence of domain-specific training data, it is difficult for LLMs to accurately interpret slang meaning based on lexical information. This paper attempts to investigate the challenges of slang inference using large LLMs and presents a greedy search-guided chain-of-thought framework for slang interpretation. Through our experiments, we conclude that the model size and temperature settings have limited impact on inference accuracy. Transformer-based models with larger active parameters do not generate higher accuracy than smaller models. Based on the results of the above empirical study, we integrate greedy search algorithms with chain-of-thought prompting for small language models to build a framework that improves the accuracy of slang interpretation. The experimental results indicate that our proposed framework demonstrates improved accuracy in slang meaning interpretation. These findings contribute to the understanding of context dependency in language models and provide a practical solution for enhancing slang comprehension through a structured reasoning prompting framework.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why large language models struggle with interpreting slang and proposes a greedy search–guided chain‑of‑thought framework that significantly improves slang meaning interpretation, especially for smaller models.
AI.motivation: Slang meaning is highly context-, culture-, and community-dependent, so standard LLMs that rely mostly on lexical statistics and lack domain-specific data often misinterpret it. Existing work focuses on scaling model size or tuning temperature, but these do not adequately solve slang inference. The authors want to understand what really affects slang interpretation performance and to design a practical method that improves it without relying on massive models or specialized training data.
AI.method: The authors conduct empirical experiments with various transformer-based LLMs of different sizes and decoding temperatures on a slang interpretation task. They analyze how model size and temperature influence accuracy. Based on the observed limitations, they design a framework that combines chain-of-thought prompting with a greedy search algorithm to guide the reasoning process of smaller language models when inferring slang meaning. This framework structures the model’s reasoning steps and uses greedy search to iteratively choose the most promising intermediate interpretations.
AI.result: The experiments show that increasing model size or adjusting temperature has only limited effect on slang interpretation accuracy, and larger transformer models with more active parameters do not reliably outperform smaller ones. In contrast, the proposed greedy search–guided chain-of-thought framework for small LLMs yields higher accuracy in interpreting slang meanings compared with baseline prompting/decoding setups.
AI.conclusion: Model scale and temperature are not the main determinants of success in slang interpretation; instead, structured reasoning plays a more important role. By integrating greedy search with chain-of-thought prompting, small language models can better handle context-dependent slang, achieving improved interpretation accuracy. The work highlights the importance of context-sensitive reasoning for non-literal language and offers a practical, training-free approach to enhance slang comprehension in LLMs.

title: Steering at the Source: Style Modulation Heads for Robust Persona Control
summary: Activation steering offers a computationally efficient mechanism for controlling Large Language Models (LLMs) without fine-tuning. While effectively controlling target traits (e.g., persona), coherency degradation remains a major obstacle to safety and practical deployment. We hypothesize that this degradation stems from intervening on the residual stream, which indiscriminately affects aggregated features and inadvertently amplifies off-target noise. In this work, we identify a sparse subset of attention heads (only three heads) that independently govern persona and style formation, which we term Style Modulation Heads. Specifically, these heads can be localized via geometric analysis of internal representations, combining layer-wise cosine similarity and head-wise contribution scores. We demonstrate that intervention targeting only these specific heads achieves robust behavioral control while significantly mitigating the coherency degradation observed in residual stream steering. More broadly, our findings show that precise, component-level localization enables safer and more precise model control.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a safer, more coherent way to control LLM behavior using activation steering focused on a few key attention heads rather than the whole residual stream.
AI.motivation: Existing activation steering methods can successfully modify traits like persona or style in LLMs but often degrade output coherency, limiting safety and real-world usability. The authors suspect that steering via the residual stream is too coarse, unintentionally amplifying unrelated noise and harming generation quality. They seek a more targeted, interpretable mechanism for behavior control.
AI.method: The authors analyze internal model representations to locate attention heads most responsible for persona and style. They use layer-wise cosine similarity and head-wise contribution scores to geometrically characterize and rank heads, identifying a minimal subset they call Style Modulation Heads. They then apply activation steering interventions only to these heads instead of the full residual stream and compare behaviors and coherency to traditional steering methods.
AI.result: They find that only a sparse set of three attention heads largely governs persona and style formation. Steering these specific heads achieves strong, robust control over target traits while greatly reducing the coherency degradation seen with residual-stream-level interventions. The experiments show that head-level localization yields more precise and stable control of model behavior.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that effective and safe LLM control can be achieved through fine-grained, component-level activation steering focused on a few key attention heads. By localizing Style Modulation Heads, they show that it is possible to modulate persona and style with minimal side effects, suggesting a general strategy for more interpretable and reliable LLM control techniques.

title: Training-Free Agentic AI: Probabilistic Control and Coordination in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems enable complex, long-horizon reasoning by composing specialized agents, but practical deployment remains hindered by inefficient routing, noisy feedback, and high interaction cost. We introduce REDEREF, a lightweight and training-free controller for multi-agent LLM collaboration that improves routing efficiency during recursive delegation. REDEREF integrates (i) belief-guided delegation via Thompson sampling to prioritize agents with historically positive marginal contributions, (ii) reflection-driven re-routing using a calibrated LLM or programmatic judge, (iii) evidence-based selection rather than output averaging, and (iv) memory-aware priors to reduce cold-start inefficiency. Across multi-agent split-knowledge tasks, we show that while recursive retry alone saturates task success, belief-guided routing reduces token usage by 28%, agent calls by 17%, and time-to-success by 19% compared to random recursive delegation, and adapts gracefully under agent or judge degradation. These results demonstrate that simple, interpretable probabilistic control can meaningfully improve the efficiency and robustness of multi-agent LLM systems without training or fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: REDEREF is a lightweight, training-free probabilistic controller that makes multi-agent LLM collaborations more efficient and robust by smarter routing and selection of agents.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems can perform complex, long-horizon reasoning by delegating subtasks to specialized agents, but they suffer from inefficient random or heuristic routing, noisy or unreliable feedback, and high costs from many agent interactions and retries. There is a need for a simple, interpretable controller that can coordinate agents more intelligently, improving efficiency and robustness without requiring additional training or fine-tuning.
AI.method: The paper proposes REDEREF, a controller that manages recursive delegation among multiple LLM agents. Its core components are: (i) belief-guided delegation via Thompson sampling, which maintains beliefs over each agent’s marginal contribution and preferentially routes future queries to historically better-performing agents; (ii) reflection-driven re-routing, where a calibrated LLM or a programmatic judge evaluates intermediate outputs and can trigger rerouting to alternative agents; (iii) evidence-based selection, which chooses a final answer based on explicit supporting evidence instead of averaging or ensembling raw outputs; and (iv) memory-aware priors that incorporate past performance and context to mitigate cold-start inefficiency for new or rarely used agents. The controller operates in a training-free fashion, adding a probabilistic control layer on top of existing agents and judges.
AI.result: On multi-agent split-knowledge benchmarks—tasks that require coordination across specialized agents—the authors find that naive recursive retry saturates task success but at high cost. REDEREF’s belief-guided routing achieves comparable success with better efficiency: 28% fewer tokens, 17% fewer agent calls, and 19% faster time-to-success relative to random recursive delegation. The system also shows robustness, adapting gracefully when some agents or the judge are degraded, maintaining performance with adjusted routing decisions.
AI.conclusion: Simple, interpretable probabilistic control—implemented via REDEREF—can substantially improve the efficiency and robustness of multi-agent LLM systems without extra training or fine-tuning. By using belief-guided routing, reflection-based rerouting, evidence-aware selection, and memory-informed priors, multi-agent collaborations can maintain success rates while reducing computational and interaction costs, making such systems more practical for real-world deployment.

title: How Transformers Reject Wrong Answers: Rotational Dynamics of Factual Constraint Processing
summary:
When a language model is fed a wrong answer, what happens inside the network? Current understanding treats truthfulness as a static property of individual-layer representations-a direction to be probed, a feature to be extracted. Less is known about the dynamics: how internal representations diverge across the full depth of the network when the model processes correct versus incorrect continuations.
  We introduce forced-completion probing, a method that presents identical queries with known correct and incorrect single-token continuations and tracks five geometric measurements across every layer of four decoder-only models(1.5B-13B parameters). We report three findings. First, correct and incorrect paths diverge through rotation, not rescaling: displacement vectors maintain near-identical magnitudes while their angular separation increases, meaning factual selection is encoded in direction on an approximate hypersphere. Second, the model does not passively fail on incorrect input-it actively suppresses the correct answer, driving internal probability away from the right token. Third, both phenomena are entirely absent below a parameter threshold and emerge at 1.6B, suggesting a phase transition in factual processing capability.
  These results show that factual constraint processing has a specific geometric character-rotational, not scalar; active, not passive-that is invisible to methods based on single-layer probes or magnitude comparisons.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how language models internally process correct vs. incorrect answers, finding that factual correctness is represented as directional differences that evolve across layers, rather than as simple magnitude changes.
AI.motivation: Existing work mainly treats truthfulness as a static feature in single layers (e.g., a probe direction), leaving open how representations evolve across the entire depth of a network when it is given right versus wrong continuations. The authors want to understand the dynamics and geometry of factual processing inside language models, and whether the model passively fails on wrong inputs or actively suppresses correct answers.
AI.method: They propose forced-completion probing: feed the model the same prompt but force it to continue with either the known-correct or a known-incorrect single token. Then, for four decoder-only models ranging from 1.5B to 13B parameters, they track five geometric quantities (e.g., vector norms, angles, and related measures) between the internal representations along the correct vs. incorrect computation paths at every layer.
AI.result: They find: (1) The internal paths for correct vs. incorrect answers diverge mainly through rotation in representation space, not changes in vector magnitude—displacement vectors keep similar norms while their angular separation increases, implying factual information is encoded in direction on an approximate hypersphere. (2) When forced to process an incorrect continuation, the model does not just fail to predict the right token; it actively steers probability mass away from the correct answer, effectively suppressing it. (3) These geometric and probabilistic behaviors only appear above a model size threshold, emerging around 1.6B parameters, consistent with a phase transition in factual processing ability.
AI.conclusion: Factual constraint processing in language models has a distinct geometric signature: it is directional/rotational rather than magnitude-based, and involves active suppression of correct answers on incorrect inputs. These dynamics span multiple layers and are missed by analyses that focus only on single-layer probes or vector norms. The work implies that understanding truthfulness and factual reasoning in LMs requires multi-layer, geometric, and dynamical analysis, and that such behaviors emerge only beyond a certain scale.

title: Human Attribution of Causality to AI Across Agency, Misuse, and Misalignment
summary: AI-related incidents are becoming increasingly frequent and severe, ranging from safety failures to misuse by malicious actors. In such complex situations, identifying which elements caused an adverse outcome, the problem of cause selection, is a critical first step for establishing liability. This paper investigates folk perceptions of causal responsibility in causal chain structures when AI systems are involved in harmful outcomes. We conduct human experiments to examine judgments of causality, blame, foreseeability, and counterfactual reasoning. Our findings show that: (1) When AI agency was moderate (human sets the goal, AI determines the means) or high (AI sets the goal and the means), participants attributed greater causal responsibility to the AI. However, under low AI agency (where a human sets both a goal and means) participants assigned greater causal responsibility to the human despite their temporal distance from the outcome and despite both agents intended it, suggesting an effect of autonomy; (2) When we reversed roles between human and AI, participants consistently judged the human as more causal, even when both agents perform the same action; (3) The developer, despite being distant in the chain, was judged highly causal, reducing causal attributions to the human user but not to the AI; (4) Decomposing the AI into a large language model and an agentic component showed that the agentic part was judged as more causal in the chain. Overall, our research provides evidence on how people perceive the causal contribution of AI in both misuse and misalignment scenarios, and how these judgments interact with the roles of users and developers, key actors in assigning responsibility. These findings can inform the design of liability frameworks for AI-caused harms and shed light on how intuitive judgments shape social and policy debates surrounding real-world AI-related incidents.

AI.tldr: The paper experimentally studies how people intuitively assign causal responsibility to AI systems, human users, and developers in harmful AI outcomes, finding that perceived AI autonomy, human-vs-AI role, and developer involvement systematically shift causal attributions.
AI.motivation: As AI incidents grow, legal and policy debates need to know whom people see as causally responsible—AI systems, users, or developers—because these folk intuitions influence liability frameworks, regulation, and public acceptance. Yet we lack systematic evidence on how people perceive causal responsibility along complex chains where AI has varying degrees of autonomy and where multiple human actors are involved.
AI.method: The authors design and run human-subject experiments using vignettes that describe harmful outcomes produced by AI systems under different causal chain structures. They manipulate: (a) the level of AI agency (who sets goals and selects means), (b) whether humans or AIs occupy particular roles in the chain, (c) the presence and distance of the developer, and (d) whether the AI is represented as a monolithic system or decomposed into a large language model plus an agentic layer. Participants then rate causality, blame, foreseeability, and counterfactual dependence for each actor in the scenario.
AI.result: (1) Higher perceived AI agency (AI choosing means or both goals and means) increases attributions of causal responsibility to the AI, whereas with low AI agency people see the human as more causally responsible despite temporal distance and similar intentions, revealing an autonomy effect. (2) When human and AI roles are swapped but actions are held constant, participants still regard the human as more causally responsible, indicating an asymmetry favoring human causality. (3) Developers are judged highly causally responsible even though they are temporally remote, which diminishes causal attributions to the human user but not to the AI. (4) When an AI is decomposed into an LLM plus an agentic component, the agentic part is seen as more causally responsible than the LLM component.
AI.conclusion: People’s causal responsibility judgments for AI-related harms are systematically shaped by perceived autonomy, human-versus-AI role, and the presence of developers, with agentic AI components bearing more perceived responsibility than underlying models. These patterns suggest that liability and governance frameworks should account for folk intuitions about agency and role, especially regarding developers and agentic layers, because such intuitions will influence acceptance and perceived fairness of AI accountability regimes.

title: Explain in Your Own Words: Improving Reasoning via Token-Selective Dual Knowledge Distillation
comment: The Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) 2026, Accepted
summary: Knowledge Distillation (KD) can transfer the reasoning abilities of large models to smaller ones, which can reduce the costs to generate Chain-of-Thoughts for reasoning tasks. KD methods typically ask the student to mimic the teacher's distribution over the entire output. However, a student with limited capacity can be overwhelmed by such extensive supervision causing a distribution mismatch, especially in complex reasoning tasks. We propose Token-Selective Dual Knowledge Distillation (TSD-KD), a framework for student-centric distillation. TSD-KD focuses on distilling important tokens for reasoning and encourages the student to explain reasoning in its own words. TSD-KD combines indirect and direct distillation. Indirect distillation uses a weak form of feedback based on preference ranking. The student proposes candidate responses generated on its own; the teacher re-ranks those candidates as indirect feedback without enforcing its entire distribution. Direct distillation uses distribution matching; however, it selectively distills tokens based on the relative confidence between teacher and student. Finally, we add entropy regularization to maintain the student's confidence during distillation. Overall, our method provides the student with targeted and indirect feedback to support its own reasoning process and to facilitate self-improvement. The experiments show the state-of-the-art performance of TSD-KD on 10 challenging reasoning benchmarks, outperforming the baseline and runner-up in accuracy by up to 54.4\% and 40.3\%, respectively. Notably, a student trained by TSD-KD even outperformed its own teacher model in four cases by up to 20.3\%. The source code is available at https://github.com/kmswin1/TSD-KD.

AI.tldr: They introduce Token-Selective Dual Knowledge Distillation (TSD-KD), a student-centric distillation method that selectively distills key reasoning tokens and uses both preference-based and distribution-based supervision, achieving state-of-the-art results and sometimes exceeding the teacher model.
AI.motivation: Standard knowledge distillation forces a small student model to imitate the full output distribution of a large teacher model. In complex reasoning and chain-of-thought generation, this overwhelms low-capacity students, causing distribution mismatch and poor transfer of reasoning ability. There is a need for a distillation method that better matches the student’s capacity and focuses on what really matters for reasoning.
AI.method: They propose TSD-KD, which is ‘student-centric’ and has two complementary components plus regularization. (1) Indirect distillation: the student generates multiple candidate responses on its own; the teacher does not impose its full distribution but instead re-ranks these candidates, providing preference-based feedback (weak supervision). (2) Direct distillation: they still use distribution matching but only for selected tokens that are important for reasoning, determined via relative confidence between teacher and student; this avoids overwhelming the student with unnecessary supervision. (3) Entropy regularization is added to keep the student’s confidence calibrated and prevent overconfident or collapsed distributions during training.
AI.result: On 10 difficult reasoning benchmarks, TSD-KD yields state-of-the-art performance. It improves accuracy by as much as 54.4% over a basic baseline and 40.3% over the previous runner-up method. In four benchmarks, a student trained with TSD-KD even surpasses its teacher model by up to 20.3% accuracy, indicating effective and possibly improved reasoning after distillation.
AI.conclusion: Focusing distillation on key reasoning tokens and combining indirect (preference ranking) with selective direct distribution matching yields a more effective, student-centric distillation process for reasoning tasks. This targeted and less intrusive supervision allows smaller models to develop their own reasoning style, maintain appropriate confidence, and in some cases even outperform the original teacher model.

title: A Dual-Path Generative Framework for Zero-Day Fraud Detection in Banking Systems
summary: High-frequency banking environments face a critical trade-off between low-latency fraud detection and the regulatory explainability demanded by GDPR. Traditional rule-based and discriminative models struggle with "zero-day" attacks due to extreme class imbalance and the lack of historical precedents. This paper proposes a Dual-Path Generative Framework that decouples real-time anomaly detection from offline adversarial training. The architecture employs a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to establish a legitimate transaction manifold based on reconstruction error, ensuring <50ms inference latency. In parallel, an asynchronous Wasserstein GAN with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP) synthesizes high-entropy fraudulent scenarios to stress-test the detection boundaries. Crucially, to address the non-differentiability of discrete banking data (e.g., Merchant Category Codes), we integrate a Gumbel-Softmax estimator. Furthermore, we introduce a trigger-based explainability mechanism where SHAP (Shapley Additive Explanations) is activated only for high-uncertainty transactions, reconciling the computational cost of XAI with real-time throughput requirements.

AI.tldr: Dual-path system for low-latency, explainable fraud detection in high-frequency banking using VAE for anomalies and WGAN-GP with Gumbel-Softmax for adversarial training, plus selective SHAP-based explanations.
AI.motivation: In high-frequency banking, fraud must be detected with extremely low latency while still meeting regulatory demands for explainability. Existing rule-based and discriminative models fail especially on novel, zero-day fraud due to heavy class imbalance and lack of labeled fraudulent examples. There is a need for a system that can generalize to unseen attacks, remain fast enough for production, and still provide explanations when required by regulations such as GDPR.
AI.method: They propose a Dual-Path Generative Framework with two decoupled components: (1) an online Variational Autoencoder (VAE) trained on legitimate transactions that detects anomalies via reconstruction error within <50 ms, and (2) an asynchronous offline Wasserstein GAN with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP) that generates high-entropy fraudulent transaction scenarios for adversarial stress-testing and boundary refinement. To handle discrete banking features like Merchant Category Codes in the generative modeling, they incorporate a Gumbel-Softmax estimator for differentiable sampling. For explainability, they implement a trigger-based mechanism where SHAP explanations are computed only for high-uncertainty or borderline transactions, reducing the computational overhead while still providing explanations when most needed.
AI.result: The abstract implies that the framework achieves low-latency inference (<50ms) while enabling robust detection of anomalous, potentially zero-day fraudulent transactions and providing on-demand explanations via SHAP. The WGAN-GP component enriches the fraud space, allowing more resilient decision boundaries, and the Gumbel-Softmax makes it feasible to model discrete transaction attributes within the generative pipeline.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-path generative architecture successfully reconciles the trade-off between speed and regulatory explainability in high-frequency fraud detection. By separating real-time anomaly detection from offline adversarial training and using selective SHAP-based triggers for explainability, the system appears better suited for handling zero-day fraud under strict latency and transparency constraints compared to traditional rule-based or purely discriminative approaches.

title: Design and evaluation of an agentic workflow for crisis-related synthetic tweet datasets
summary: Twitter (now X) has become an important source of social media data for situational awareness during crises. Crisis informatics research has widely used tweets from Twitter to develop and evaluate artificial intelligence (AI) systems for various crisis-relevant tasks, such as extracting locations and estimating damage levels from tweets to support damage assessment. However, recent changes in Twitter's data access policies have made it increasingly difficult to curate real-world tweet datasets related to crises. Moreover, existing curated tweet datasets are limited to past crisis events in specific contexts and are costly to annotate at scale. These limitations constrain the development and evaluation of AI systems used in crisis informatics. To address these limitations, we introduce an agentic workflow for generating crisis-related synthetic tweet datasets. The workflow iteratively generates synthetic tweets conditioned on prespecified target characteristics, evaluates them using predefined compliance checks, and incorporates structured feedback to refine them in subsequent iterations. As a case study, we apply the workflow to generate synthetic tweet datasets relevant to post-earthquake damage assessment. We show that the workflow can generate synthetic tweets that capture their target labels for location and damage level. We further demonstrate that the resulting synthetic tweet datasets can be used to evaluate AI systems on damage assessment tasks like geolocalization and damage level prediction. Our results indicate that the workflow offers a flexible and scalable alternative to real-world tweet data curation, enabling the systematic generation of synthetic social media data across diverse crisis events, societal contexts, and crisis informatics applications.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an agentic workflow to generate synthetic crisis-related tweets as a scalable alternative to real-world Twitter data for training and evaluating AI systems in crisis informatics.
AI.motivation: Access to real-world Twitter data for crisis informatics has become increasingly restricted, and existing crisis tweet datasets are limited, context-specific, and expensive to annotate, which hinders the development and evaluation of AI systems for tasks such as damage assessment.
AI.method: The authors design an iterative agentic workflow that: (1) generates synthetic tweets conditioned on predefined target characteristics (e.g., location, damage level), (2) evaluates the generated tweets using compliance checks, and (3) incorporates structured feedback to refine subsequent generations. They apply this workflow to the domain of post-earthquake damage assessment.
AI.result: The workflow produces synthetic tweets that align well with target labels for location and damage level. These synthetic datasets are successfully used to evaluate AI systems on downstream crisis-relevant tasks such as geolocalization and damage level prediction.
AI.conclusion: An agentic workflow for generating synthetic crisis-related tweets can serve as a flexible, scalable alternative to curating real-world Twitter data, enabling systematic creation of labeled datasets for diverse crisis events, contexts, and applications in crisis informatics.

title: Benchmarking Zero-Shot Reasoning Approaches for Error Detection in Solidity Smart Contracts
summary: Smart contracts play a central role in blockchain systems by encoding financial and operational logic. Still, their susceptibility to subtle security flaws poses significant risks of financial loss and erosion of trust. LLMs create new opportunities for automating vulnerability detection, yet the effectiveness of different prompting strategies and model choices in real-world contexts remains uncertain. This paper evaluates state-of-the-art LLMs on Solidity smart contract analysis using a balanced dataset of 400 contracts under two tasks: (i) Error Detection, where the model performs binary classification to decide whether a contract is vulnerable, and (ii) Error Classification, where the model must assign the predicted issue to a specific vulnerability category. Models are evaluated using zero-shot prompting strategies, including zero-shot, zero-shot Chain-of-Thought (CoT), and zero-shot Tree-of-Thought (ToT). In the Error Detection task, CoT and ToT substantially increase recall (often approaching $\approx 95$--$99\%$), but typically reduce precision, indicating a more sensitive decision regime with more false positives. In the Error Classification task, Claude 3 Opus attains the best Weighted F1-score (90.8) under the ToT prompt, followed closely by its CoT.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks modern LLMs on detecting and categorizing vulnerabilities in Solidity smart contracts, comparing zero-shot, Chain-of-Thought, and Tree-of-Thought prompting.
AI.motivation: Smart contracts are safety-critical and vulnerable to subtle bugs, which can cause heavy financial losses. While LLMs seem promising for automated security analysis, it is unclear which models and prompting strategies work best for realistic vulnerability detection and classification on Solidity contracts.
AI.method: The authors build a balanced dataset of 400 Solidity smart contracts and define two tasks: (1) Error Detection (binary classification: vulnerable vs. not vulnerable) and (2) Error Classification (assigning a vulnerability category). They evaluate multiple state-of-the-art LLMs with several zero-shot prompting strategies: basic zero-shot, zero-shot Chain-of-Thought, and zero-shot Tree-of-Thought, then compare metrics like recall, precision, and Weighted F1-score.
AI.result: For Error Detection, CoT and ToT prompting dramatically improve recall (often around 95–99%) but at the cost of reduced precision, implying more false positives. For Error Classification, Claude 3 Opus with a Tree-of-Thought prompt achieves the highest Weighted F1-score (90.8), with its CoT performance being similar and close behind.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-style prompts (CoT/ToT) make LLMs more sensitive in detecting vulnerabilities, boosting recall but sacrificing precision in detection, while also improving fine-grained vulnerability classification. Among evaluated models, Claude 3 Opus with ToT prompting performs best overall on the classification task.

title: Widespread Gender and Pronoun Bias in Moral Judgments Across LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to assess moral or ethical statements, yet their judgments may reflect social and linguistic biases. This work presents a controlled, sentence-level study of how grammatical person, number, and gender markers influence LLM moral classifications of fairness. Starting from 550 balanced base sentences from the ETHICS dataset, we generated 26 counterfactual variants per item, systematically varying pronouns and demographic markers to yield 14,850 semantically equivalent sentences. We evaluated six model families (Grok, GPT, LLaMA, Gemma, DeepSeek, and Mistral), and measured fairness judgments and inter-group disparities using Statistical Parity Difference (SPD). Results show statistically significant biases: sentences written in the singular form and third person are more often judged as "fair'', while those in the second person are penalized. Gender markers produce the strongest effects, with non-binary subjects consistently favored and male subjects disfavored. We conjecture that these patterns reflect distributional and alignment biases learned during training, emphasizing the need for targeted fairness interventions in moral LLM applications.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how grammatical person, number, and gender markers in sentences influence LLMs’ moral fairness judgments, revealing systematic biases across models.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are increasingly used for moral or ethical evaluations, their outputs may be biased by superficial linguistic cues rather than underlying semantics. Understanding whether simple changes like pronouns or gender markers systematically shift fairness judgments is crucial for building trustworthy moral reasoning systems and preventing hidden discrimination in downstream applications.
AI.method: The authors start from 550 base moral statements from the ETHICS dataset and generate 26 counterfactual variants for each, systematically changing grammatical person (first/second/third), number (singular/plural), and gender or demographic markers. This yields 14,850 semantically equivalent sentences. They then query six LLM families (Grok, GPT, LLaMA, Gemma, DeepSeek, and Mistral) for fairness judgments on each variant and quantify group-level differences using Statistical Parity Difference (SPD), comparing how often each form is labeled as “fair.”
AI.result: Across all tested model families, grammatical and demographic markers significantly affect fairness judgments despite identical semantics. Singular and third-person formulations are more frequently judged “fair,” whereas second-person sentences are more often penalized. Gender markers have the largest effect: non-binary subjects are systematically more likely to receive fair judgments, while male subjects are relatively disfavored. These effects are robust and statistically significant according to SPD analyses.
AI.conclusion: LLMs’ moral classifications are not invariant to superficial linguistic changes; they encode systematic, measurable biases tied to person, number, and especially gender markers. The authors argue these patterns likely stem from distributional properties of the training data and from alignment processes that overcorrect or skew sensitivities to certain identities. They highlight the need for targeted fairness interventions and evaluation protocols when deploying LLMs in moral or ethical decision-making contexts.

title: Benchmarking Large Language Models on Reference Extraction and Parsing in the Social Sciences and Humanities
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at the SCOLIA 2026 Workshop (Second Workshop on Scholarly Information Access), co-located with ECIR 2026. Workshop date: April 2, 2026
summary: Bibliographic reference extraction and parsing are foundational for citation indexing, linking, and downstream scholarly knowledge-graph construction. However, most established evaluations focus on clean, English, end-of-document bibliographies, and therefore underrepresent the Social Sciences and Humanities (SSH), where citations are frequently multilingual, embedded in footnotes, abbreviated, and shaped by heterogeneous historical conventions. We present a unified benchmark that targets these SSH-realistic conditions across three complementary datasets: CEX (English journal articles spanning multiple disciplines), EXCITE (German/English documents with end-section, footnote-only, and mixed regimes), and LinkedBooks (humanities references with strong stylistic variation and multilinguality). We evaluate three tasks of increasing difficulty -- reference extraction, reference parsing, and end-to-end document parsing -- under a schema-constrained setup that enables direct comparison between a strong supervised pipeline baseline (GROBID) and contemporary LLMs (DeepSeek-V3.1, Mistral-Small-3.2-24B, Gemma-3-27B-it, and Qwen3-VL (4B-32B variants)). Across datasets, extraction largely saturates beyond a moderate capability threshold, while parsing and end-to-end parsing remain the primary bottlenecks due to structured-output brittleness under noisy layouts. We further show that lightweight LoRA adaptation yields consistent gains -- especially on SSH-heavy benchmarks -- and that segmentation/pipelining can substantially improve robustness. Finally, we argue for hybrid deployment via routing: leveraging GROBID for well-structured, in-distribution PDFs while escalating multilingual and footnote-heavy documents to task-adapted LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a benchmark and evaluation comparing traditional supervised tools and modern LLMs for extracting and parsing bibliographic references in realistic Social Sciences and Humanities scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of bibliographic reference extraction/parsing assume clean, English, end-of-document references, which poorly represent Social Sciences and Humanities where citations are often multilingual, embedded in footnotes, abbreviated, and historically heterogeneous. There is a need for a unified, SSH-realistic benchmark and a fair comparison between traditional pipeline systems and LLMs under a shared schema.
AI.method: The authors construct a unified benchmark comprising three datasets: CEX (multi-disciplinary English journal articles), EXCITE (German/English documents with varied citation regimes including footnote-only), and LinkedBooks (humanities references with strong stylistic variation and multilinguality). They define three tasks of increasing difficulty: (1) reference extraction, (2) reference parsing, and (3) end-to-end document parsing. Under a schema-constrained evaluation, they compare a strong supervised pipeline baseline (GROBID) to several contemporary LLMs (DeepSeek-V3.1, Mistral-Small-3.2-24B, Gemma-3-27B-it, Qwen3-VL 4B–32B). They also study the impact of lightweight LoRA fine-tuning and segmentation/pipelining strategies, and explore routing strategies that combine GROBID and LLMs based on document characteristics.
AI.result: They find that reference extraction performance quickly saturates once models reach a moderate capability level, whereas reference parsing and full end-to-end parsing remain difficult, primarily due to the brittleness of producing structured outputs from noisy document layouts. LoRA adaptation consistently improves performance, particularly on SSH-heavy benchmarks. Segmentation and pipelined processing substantially improve robustness. The experiments indicate that different systems excel in different document regimes, motivating hybrid deployment strategies.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that realistic SSH conditions expose significant remaining challenges in reference parsing and end-to-end bibliographic processing, even as extraction itself is largely solved above a certain capability threshold. Lightweight task adaptation and better pipelining improve robustness, and a practical path forward is a hybrid routing approach: use GROBID for relatively clean, well-structured PDFs and escalate difficult, multilingual, or footnote-heavy documents to task-adapted LLMs. The benchmark offers a more representative way to evaluate systems serving the Social Sciences and Humanities.

title: Automating Document Intelligence in Statutory City Planning
comment: Accepted for presentation at Computing Conference 2026
summary: UK planning authorities face a legislative conflict between the Planning Act, which mandates public access to application documents, and the Data Protection Act, which requires protection of personal information. This situation creates a manually intensive workload for processing large document volumes, diverting planning officers to administrative tasks and creating legal compliance risks. This paper presents an integrated AI system designed to address these challenges. The system automates the identification and redaction of personal information, extracts key metadata from planning documents, and analyzes architectural drawings for specified features. It operates with an AI-in-the-Loop (AI2L) design, presenting all suggestions for review and confirmation by planning officers directly within their existing software; no action is committed without explicit human approval. The system is designed to improve its performance over time by learning from this human oversight through active learning prioritization rather than autoapproval. The system is currently being piloted at four diverse UK local authorities. The paper details the system design, the AI2L workflow, and the evaluation framework used in the pilot. Additionally, it describes a preliminary Return on Investment (ROI) model developed to quantify potential savings and secure partner participation. This work provides a case study on deploying AI to reduce administrative burden and manage compliance risk in a public sector environment.

AI.tldr: AI system for UK planning authorities to automatically redact personal data, extract metadata, and analyze drawings, with human-in-the-loop oversight and ROI-focused pilot evaluation.
AI.motivation: UK planning authorities must both publish planning documents and protect personal data, creating legal tension, heavy manual workloads, and compliance risk. There is a need to automate document processing while maintaining legal and procedural control.
AI.method: Design and deployment of an integrated AI-in-the-loop (AI2L) system that: (1) detects and redacts personal information; (2) extracts key metadata from planning documents; (3) analyzes architectural drawings for predefined features. All AI outputs are presented inside existing planning software for human review and explicit approval, with active-learning-based improvement over time. A pilot across four UK local authorities with an accompanying evaluation and ROI modelling framework.
AI.result: The abstract reports system design, implemented AI2L workflow, and an evaluation framework, along with a preliminary ROI model. Concrete quantitative performance or savings are not yet reported in the abstract; the system is under pilot at four local authorities.
AI.conclusion: An AI-in-the-loop system can realistically be integrated into public sector planning workflows to reduce administrative burden and manage compliance risk, while keeping humans in control and providing a structured way to reason about costs and benefits through ROI modelling.

title: Privacy Preserving Topic-wise Sentiment Analysis of the Iran Israel USA Conflict Using Federated Transformer Models
summary: The recent escalation of the Iran Israel USA conflict in 2026 has triggered widespread global discussions across social media platforms. As people increasingly use these platforms for expressing opinions, analyzing public sentiment from these discussions can provide valuable insights into global public perception. This study aims to analyze global public sentiment regarding the Iran Israel USA conflict by mining user-generated comments from YouTube news channels. The work contributes to public opinion analysis by introducing a privacy preserving framework that combines topic wise sentiment analysis with modern deep learning techniques and Federated Learning. To achieve this, approximately 19,000 YouTube comments were collected from major international news channels and preprocessed to remove noise and normalize text. Sentiment labels were initially generated using the VADER sentiment analyzer and later validated through manual inspection to improve reliability. Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) was applied to identify key discussion topics related to the conflict. Several transformer-based models, including BERT, RoBERTa, XLNet, DistilBERT, ModernBERT, and ELECTRA, were fine tuned for sentiment classification. The best-performing model was further integrated into a federated learning environment to enable distributed training by preserving user data privacy. Additionally, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) techniques using SHAP were applied to interpret model predictions and identify influential words affecting sentiment classification. Experimental results demonstrate that transformer models perform effectively, and among them, ELECTRA achieved the best performance with 91.32% accuracy. The federated learning also maintained strong performance while preserving privacy, achieving 89.59% accuracy in a two client configuration.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes global public sentiment on the 2026 Iran–Israel–USA conflict using YouTube comments, combining topic modeling, transformer-based sentiment classification, federated learning for privacy, and XAI for interpretability.
AI.motivation: With rising geopolitical tensions and widespread use of social media, there is a need to understand global public sentiment while respecting user privacy. Existing sentiment analysis systems often ignore privacy and interpretability, and may not leverage the latest deep learning and federated learning techniques in a unified framework.
AI.method: Roughly 19,000 YouTube comments about the conflict were collected and preprocessed. Initial sentiment labels were created using VADER and then manually checked. LDA was used to extract discussion topics. Several transformer models (BERT, RoBERTa, XLNet, DistilBERT, ModernBERT, ELECTRA) were fine-tuned for sentiment classification. The best model (ELECTRA) was embedded in a federated learning setup to enable decentralized training without centralizing raw data. SHAP-based XAI methods were applied to explain model predictions and identify influential tokens.
AI.result: Transformer models yielded strong performance; ELECTRA performed best with 91.32% accuracy in centralized training. When moved to a two-client federated learning setting, the model maintained high performance with 89.59% accuracy. Topic modeling successfully revealed key themes in the conversation, and SHAP explanations highlighted which words most strongly drove sentiment predictions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively captures public sentiment on the conflict from social media while preserving user privacy through federated learning and improving transparency via XAI. ELECTRA is identified as a particularly effective backbone model. The study shows that high-accuracy sentiment analysis, privacy preservation, and interpretability can be combined in a single practical system for large-scale opinion monitoring.

title: Multi-Axis Trust Modeling for Interpretable Account Hijacking Detection
summary: This paper proposes a Hadith-inspired multi-axis trust modeling framework, motivated by a structurally analogous problem in classical Hadith scholarship: assessing the trustworthiness of information sources using interpretable, multidimensional criteria rather than a single anomaly score. We translate five trust axes - long-term integrity (adalah), behavioral precision (dabt), contextual continuity (isnad), cumulative reputation, and anomaly evidence - into a compact set of 26 semantically meaningful behavioral features for user accounts. In addition, we introduce lightweight temporal features that capture short-horizon changes in these trust signals across consecutive activity windows. We evaluate the framework on the CLUE-LDS cloud activity dataset with injected account hijacking scenarios. On 23,094 sliding windows, a Random Forest trained on the trust features achieves near-perfect detection performance, substantially outperforming models based on raw event counts, minimal statistical baselines, and unsupervised anomaly detection. Temporal features provide modest but consistent gains on CLUE-LDS, confirming their compatibility with the static trust representation. To assess robustness under more challenging conditions, we further evaluate the approach on the CERT Insider Threat Test Dataset r6.2, which exhibits extreme class imbalance and sparse malicious behavior. On a 500-user CERT subset, temporal features improve ROC-AUC from 0.776 to 0.844. On a leakage-controlled 4,000-user configuration, temporal modeling yields a substantial and consistent improvement over static trust features alone (ROC-AUC 0.627 to 0.715; PR-AUC 0.072 to 0.264).

AI.tldr: A Hadith-inspired, multi-axis trust framework is proposed for detecting compromised or malicious user accounts, using interpretable behavioral features and temporal dynamics, and it significantly outperforms baselines on cloud and insider-threat datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing account and insider-threat detection methods often rely on single anomaly scores or opaque models, which are hard to interpret and may not capture the multidimensional nature of trust and user behavior. The authors are motivated by an analogy with classical Hadith scholarship, where the trustworthiness of narrators is assessed through multiple, well-defined dimensions, and they aim to bring a similar interpretable, structured approach to user trust modeling in security analytics.
AI.method: They define five trust axes inspired by Hadith sciences—long-term integrity (adalah), behavioral precision (dabt), contextual continuity (isnad), cumulative reputation, and anomaly evidence—and operationalize them as 26 interpretable behavioral features over user accounts. They then add lightweight temporal features that quantify changes in these trust indicators across consecutive activity windows. Using these features, they train supervised machine learning models (notably Random Forests) on sliding-window representations of user activity. The models are evaluated on two datasets: the CLUE-LDS cloud activity dataset with injected account hijacking scenarios, and the CERT Insider Threat Test Dataset r6.2, under different user-population and leakage-controlled configurations.
AI.result: On the CLUE-LDS dataset with 23,094 sliding windows, a Random Forest trained on the proposed trust features achieves near-perfect detection of hijacked accounts and clearly outperforms baselines based on raw event counts, simple statistical metrics, and unsupervised anomaly detection. Adding temporal features yields small but consistent performance gains. On the more challenging CERT insider-threat dataset (with extreme class imbalance and sparse attacks), temporal features substantially improve performance: on a 500-user subset, ROC-AUC improves from 0.776 to 0.844, and on a leakage-controlled 4,000-user configuration, ROC-AUC rises from 0.627 to 0.715 while PR-AUC improves from 0.072 to 0.264.
AI.conclusion: A multi-axis, interpretable trust modeling framework, inspired by Hadith scholarship and enriched with temporal dynamics, is effective for detecting compromised and malicious user accounts across different security datasets. The structured trust representation outperforms simpler baselines, and temporal modeling consistently enhances robustness and detection quality, especially in highly imbalanced, realistic insider-threat scenarios.

title: Preconditioned Test-Time Adaptation for Out-of-Distribution Debiasing in Narrative Generation
comment: This paper has been submitted to ACL2026 main conference
summary: Although debiased LLMs perform well on known bias patterns, they often fail to generalize to unfamiliar bias prompts, producing toxic outputs. We first validate that such high-bias prompts constitute a \emph{distribution shift} via OOD detection, and show static models degrade under this shift. To adapt on-the-fly, we propose \textbf{CAP-TTA}, a test-time adaptation framework that performs context-aware LoRA updates only when the bias-risk \emph{trigger} exceeds a threshold, using a precomputed diagonal \emph{preconditioner} for fast and stable updates. Across toxic-prompt settings and benchmarks, CAP-TTA reduces bias (confirmed by human evaluation) while achieving much lower update latency than AdamW/SGD; it also mitigates catastrophic forgetting by significantly improving narrative fluency over SOTA debiasing baseline while maintaining comparable debiasing effectiveness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CAP-TTA, a test-time adaptation method that dynamically updates a debiased LLM only when high bias risk is detected, improving robustness to unseen toxic prompts with low latency and limited forgetting.
AI.motivation: Debiased LLMs usually perform well on seen or known bias patterns but tend to fail on unfamiliar, high-bias prompts, generating toxic content. This indicates a distribution shift problem where static debiasing methods are insufficient. The authors want a way for models to adapt on-the-fly to out-of-distribution, high-bias prompts while preserving helpfulness and fluency.
AI.method: They first empirically validate that high-bias prompts represent a distribution shift using out-of-distribution (OOD) detection techniques and show that static LLMs degrade under this shift. Then they propose CAP-TTA, a test-time adaptation framework that (1) computes a bias-risk trigger for each input, (2) only updates the model when this trigger exceeds a threshold, and (3) performs fast, context-aware LoRA-based parameter updates using a precomputed diagonal preconditioner for stability and efficiency. They compare against standard optimizers like AdamW and SGD.
AI.result: On various toxic-prompt settings and benchmarks, CAP-TTA reduces bias and toxicity, as validated by human evaluation, while achieving significantly lower update latency compared to AdamW and SGD. It also mitigates catastrophic forgetting: narrative fluency is significantly improved over state-of-the-art debiasing baselines while maintaining comparable debiasing effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, test-time debiasing via CAP-TTA is more robust to unseen toxic prompts than static debiasing approaches. By triggering lightweight LoRA updates only when bias risk is high and using an efficient preconditioner, the method reduces toxicity, is computationally efficient, and better preserves general language quality compared to existing debiasing methods.

title: ILION: Deterministic Pre-Execution Safety Gates for Agentic AI Systems
comment: 16 pages, 7 tables, 7 figures. Benchmark and code available at https://github.com/Athonitul/ilion-framework-simulator. Patent application A/00052, OSIM Romania
summary: The proliferation of autonomous AI agents capable of executing real-world actions - filesystem operations, API calls, database modifications, financial transactions - introduces a class of safety risk not addressed by existing content-moderation infrastructure. Current text-safety systems evaluate linguistic content for harm categories such as violence, hate speech, and sexual content; they are architecturally unsuitable for evaluating whether a proposed action falls within an agent's authorized operational scope. We present ILION (Intelligent Logic Identity Operations Network), a deterministic execution gate for agentic AI systems. ILION employs a five-component cascade architecture - Transient Identity Imprint (TII), Semantic Vector Reference Frame (SVRF), Identity Drift Control (IDC), Identity Resonance Score (IRS) and Consensus Veto Layer (CVL) - to classify proposed agent actions as BLOCK or ALLOW without statistical training or API dependencies. The system requires zero labeled data, operates in sub-millisecond latency, and produces fully interpretable verdicts. We evaluate ILION on ILION-Bench v2, a purpose-built benchmark of 380 test scenarios across eight attack categories with 39% hard-difficulty adversarial cases and a held-out development split. ILION achieves F1 = 0.8515, precision = 91.0%, and a false positive rate of 7.9% at a mean latency of 143 microseconds. Comparative evaluation against three baselines - Lakera Guard (F1 = 0.8087), OpenAI Moderation API (F1 = 0.1188), and Llama Guard 3 (F1 = 0.0105) - demonstrates that existing text-safety infrastructure systematically fails on agent execution safety tasks due to a fundamental task mismatch. ILION outperforms the best commercial baseline by 4.3 F1 points while operating 2,000 times faster with a false positive rate four times lower.

AI.tldr: ILION is a deterministic safety gate for autonomous AI agents that decides whether to block or allow real-world actions, outperforming existing text-based moderation tools on an agent-safety benchmark.
AI.motivation: Autonomous AI agents can perform real-world operations like file edits, API calls, database changes, and financial transactions, creating new safety risks not handled by current content moderation systems, which only analyze text for categories like hate or sexual content. There is a need for an execution-level safety layer that can judge whether an action is within an agent’s authorized scope, is fast, data-free, and interpretable.
AI.method: The authors design ILION, a deterministic execution gate that uses a five-stage cascade: (1) Transient Identity Imprint (TII) to capture the intended operational identity or scope of the agent; (2) Semantic Vector Reference Frame (SVRF) to represent actions and identities in a structured semantic space; (3) Identity Drift Control (IDC) to detect deviations of proposed actions from the authorized identity; (4) Identity Resonance Score (IRS) to quantitatively score how well an action matches the allowed identity; and (5) Consensus Veto Layer (CVL) to aggregate component signals into a final BLOCK or ALLOW decision. The system is rule-based, requires no training data or external APIs, and is optimized for sub-millisecond latency.
AI.result: On ILION-Bench v2 with 380 scenarios covering eight attack categories and 39% hard adversarial cases, ILION attains F1 = 0.8515, precision = 91.0%, false positive rate = 7.9%, and mean latency of 143 microseconds. Against baselines, Lakera Guard gets F1 = 0.8087, OpenAI Moderation API 0.1188, and Llama Guard 3 0.0105. ILION also has ~4.3 higher F1 than the best commercial baseline, is about 2,000x faster, and has roughly four times lower false positive rate.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic, identity- and action-scope–based execution gates like ILION are better suited than traditional text-safety moderation tools for securing autonomous AI agents. Existing moderation APIs fail largely because they are designed for linguistic harm categories instead of authorization-constrained action safety. ILION demonstrates that a non-statistical, interpretable, ultra-low-latency mechanism can significantly improve execution safety for agentic systems and suggests a new architectural direction for safeguarding real-world AI operations.

title: QuarkMedBench: A Real-World Scenario Driven Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) excel on standardized medical exams, high scores often fail to translate to high-quality responses for real-world medical queries. Current evaluations rely heavily on multiple-choice questions, failing to capture the unstructured, ambiguous, and long-tail complexities inherent in genuine user inquiries. To bridge this gap, we introduce QuarkMedBench, an ecologically valid benchmark tailored for real-world medical LLM assessment. We compiled a massive dataset spanning Clinical Care, Wellness Health, and Professional Inquiry, comprising 20,821 single-turn queries and 3,853 multi-turn sessions. To objectively evaluate open-ended answers, we propose an automated scoring framework that integrates multi-model consensus with evidence-based retrieval to dynamically generate 220,617 fine-grained scoring rubrics (~9.8 per query). During evaluation, hierarchical weighting and safety constraints structurally quantify medical accuracy, key-point coverage, and risk interception, effectively mitigating the high costs and subjectivity of human grading. Experimental results demonstrate that the generated rubrics achieve a 91.8% concordance rate with clinical expert blind audits, establishing highly dependable medical reliability. Crucially, baseline evaluations on this benchmark reveal significant performance disparities among state-of-the-art models when navigating real-world clinical nuances, highlighting the limitations of conventional exam-based metrics. Ultimately, QuarkMedBench establishes a rigorous, reproducible yardstick for measuring LLM performance on complex health issues, while its framework inherently supports dynamic knowledge updates to prevent benchmark obsolescence.

AI.tldr: Introduces QuarkMedBench, a realistic benchmark and automated rubric-based scoring framework for evaluating LLMs on real-world medical queries.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform well on standardized medical exams but often fail on real-world, unstructured, and ambiguous health queries. Existing benchmarks are dominated by multiple-choice formats that do not capture clinical nuance, long-tail cases, or safety-critical aspects of open-ended medical advice. There is a need for an ecologically valid, scalable, and objective way to measure medical LLM performance in realistic settings.
AI.method: Construct a large benchmark (QuarkMedBench) with 20,821 single-turn queries and 3,853 multi-turn sessions across Clinical Care, Wellness Health, and Professional Inquiry. Develop an automated scoring framework that combines multi-model consensus and evidence-based retrieval to dynamically generate 220,617 fine-grained scoring rubrics (~9.8 per query). Use hierarchical weighting and safety constraints to quantify medical accuracy, key-point coverage, and risk interception. Validate rubric quality via blind audits by clinical experts.
AI.result: The dynamic rubrics show 91.8% agreement with clinical expert blind audits, suggesting high reliability of the automated evaluation pipeline. Baseline experiments on various state-of-the-art LLMs show large performance differences when tested on this realistic benchmark, in contrast to their seemingly similar scores on traditional exam-style benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: QuarkMedBench provides a rigorous, reproducible, and clinically aligned benchmark for assessing LLMs on complex, real-world medical queries. Its automated rubric-generation and evidence-based evaluation framework reduces dependence on costly human grading, better captures safety and nuance than exam-style tests, and can be updated dynamically to keep pace with evolving medical knowledge, mitigating benchmark obsolescence.

title: ManiBench: A Benchmark for Testing Visual-Logic Drift and Syntactic Hallucinations in Manim Code Generation
summary: Traditional benchmarks like HumanEval and MBPP test logic and syntax effectively, but fail when code must produce dynamic, pedagogical visuals. We introduce ManiBench, a specialized benchmark evaluating LLM performance in generating Manim CE code, where temporal fidelity and version-aware API correctness are critical. ManiBench targets two key failure modes: Syntactic Hallucinations (valid Python referencing non-existent or deprecated Manim APIs) and Visual-Logic Drift (generated visuals diverging from intended mathematical logic through timing errors or missing causal relationships). The benchmark comprises 150-200 problems across five difficulty levels spanning calculus, linear algebra, probability, topology, and AI, grounded in analysis of 3Blue1Brown's ManimGL source (53,000 lines, 143 scene classes). Evaluation uses a four-tier framework measuring Executability, Version-Conflict Error Rate, Alignment Score, and Coverage Score. An open-source framework automates evaluation across multiple models and prompting strategies. Code, data and benchmark suite are available at https://github.com/nabin2004/ManiBench. and the dataset is hosted on https://huggingface.co/datasets/nabin2004/ManiBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ManiBench, a benchmark to evaluate how well LLMs generate correct, version-aware Manim CE code for dynamic math visualizations, focusing on syntax, API correctness, and faithfulness of visuals to mathematical intent.
AI.motivation: Existing code benchmarks like HumanEval and MBPP mostly test static code correctness and basic logic, but do not assess whether models can create complex, time-evolving educational animations. When LLMs generate Manim code, they often use deprecated or non-existent APIs and produce animations that visually misrepresent the underlying math. There is a need for a domain-specific benchmark that captures these unique failure modes and measures the ability of LLMs to produce pedagogically accurate, executable Manim CE animations.
AI.method: The authors construct ManiBench, a benchmark of 150–200 Manim CE coding tasks across five difficulty levels and topics (calculus, linear algebra, probability, topology, and AI). The tasks are derived from an analysis of 3Blue1Brown’s ManimGL codebase (53k lines, 143 scene classes) to reflect realistic educational animations. They define and target two main failure modes: Syntactic Hallucinations (Python code that calls non-existent/deprecated Manim APIs) and Visual-Logic Drift (animations whose timing or causal relationships misalign with the intended math). They design an automated, open-source evaluation framework that runs models on these tasks and scores outputs using four metrics: Executability, Version-Conflict Error Rate, Alignment Score, and Coverage Score. The benchmark, code, and dataset are released on GitHub and Hugging Face.
AI.result: ManiBench provides a structured way to quantify how well different LLMs and prompting strategies generate correct and pedagogically faithful Manim CE animations. Using the four-tier metrics, the framework can distinguish between code that merely runs, code that uses the right Manim APIs for the target version, and code that visually and logically matches the intended mathematical content with sufficient coverage of scene elements. The benchmark and tooling allow systematic comparison across models and prompts.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current general-purpose code benchmarks are insufficient for assessing LLM capabilities in generating dynamic mathematical visualizations. ManiBench fills this gap by focusing on version-aware Manim CE code generation and visual-logical faithfulness, highlighting two critical failure modes of LLMs in this domain. By releasing the benchmark and evaluation framework as open source, the authors aim to standardize evaluation and spur progress on models that can reliably produce high-quality, educational math animations in Manim CE.

title: Repetition Without Exclusivity: Scale Sensitivity of Referential Mechanisms in Child-Scale Language Models
summary: We present the first systematic evaluation of mutual exclusivity (ME) -- the bias to map novel words to novel referents -- in text-only language models trained on child-directed speech. We operationalise ME as referential suppression: when a familiar object is relabelled in a two-referent discourse context, ME predicts decreased probability of the labelled noun at a subsequent completion position. Three pilot findings motivate a pre-registered scale-sensitivity experiment: (1) a masked language model (BabyBERTa) is entirely insensitive to multi-sentence referential context; (2) autoregressive models show robust repetition priming -- the opposite of ME -- when familiar nouns are re-labelled; and (3) a novel context-dependence diagnostic reveals that apparent ME-like patterns with nonce tokens are fully explained by embedding similarity, not referential disambiguation. In the confirmatory experiment, we train 45 GPT-2-architecture models (2.9M, 8.9M, and 33.5M parameters; 5, 10, and 20 epochs on AO-CHILDES; 5 seeds each) and evaluate on a pre-registered ME battery. Anti-ME repetition priming is significant in all 9 cells (85-100% of items; all p < 2.4 x 10^-13). Priming attenuates with improved language modelling (Spearman rho = -0.533, p = 0.0002) but never crosses zero across a 3.8x perplexity range. The context-dependence diagnostic replicates in all 9 cells, and dose-response priming increases with repetitions in 8/9 cells (all trend p < 0.002). These findings indicate that distributional learning on child-directed speech produces repetition-based reference tracking rather than lexical exclusivity. We connect this to the grounded cognition literature and argue that referential grounding may be a necessary ingredient for ME -- an empirical claim about required input structure, not a nativist one.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether text-only language models trained on child-directed speech exhibit mutual exclusivity (tendency to map new words to new referents) and finds they do not; instead they rely on repetition-based reference tracking.
AI.motivation: Mutual exclusivity is a central phenomenon in child word learning, often taken as evidence for specialized learning biases. With the rise of distributional language models trained on realistic input such as child-directed speech, it is important to test whether the ME effect can emerge purely from text-based statistical learning without grounded reference. This addresses debates about whether ME is a learned property of input statistics or requires additional cognitive or environmental structure.
AI.method: The authors operationalize ME as referential suppression: when a familiar object is re-labelled in a two-referent discourse, ME predicts lower probability of the original noun later. They first run pilots: testing a masked LM (BabyBERTa) on multi-sentence referential context, testing autoregressive models for repetition priming versus ME when familiar nouns are relabelled, and introducing a context-dependence diagnostic using nonce tokens to separate embedding similarity from true referential disambiguation. Then, in a preregistered experiment, they train 45 GPT-2-style models of three sizes (2.9M, 8.9M, 33.5M parameters) for different epochs (5,10,20) on AO-CHILDES data, with 5 random seeds each. They evaluate these on a preregistered ME stimulus battery, measure repetition priming, context dependence, and dose-response with repetitions, and analyze effects across cells with statistical tests and correlations with perplexity.
AI.result: Across all 9 combinations of size and training epochs, the models show strong repetition priming (higher probability of repeated nouns) rather than mutual exclusivity, with 85–100% of test items and highly significant p-values (< 2.4×10^-13). The strength of priming decreases as language modeling performance improves (negative Spearman correlation between priming and perplexity), but the effect never reverses sign across a sizable perplexity range. The context-dependence diagnostic confirms that ME-like behavior with nonce tokens is fully explained by embedding similarity rather than genuine referential disambiguation in all conditions. Dose-response analyses show that priming increases with more repetitions in almost all cells (8/9).
AI.conclusion: Text-only distributional learning on child-directed speech does not yield mutual exclusivity; instead, models track reference via repetition and embedding similarity. This suggests that ME is not an emergent property of ungrounded language statistics alone. The authors argue, in line with grounded cognition accounts, that referential grounding in non-linguistic context may be a necessary ingredient for mutual exclusivity to arise, framing this as a claim about required input structure rather than an innate bias for ME.

title: When Alpha Breaks: Two-Level Uncertainty for Safe Deployment of Cross-Sectional Stock Rankers
comment: 34 pages, 14 tables. Cross-sectional equity ranking; uncertainty-based abstention and tail-risk capping under regime shifts
summary: Cross-sectional ranking models are often deployed as if point predictions were sufficient: the model outputs scores and the portfolio follows the induced ordering. Under non-stationarity, rankers can fail during regime shifts. In the AI Stock Forecaster, a LightGBM ranker performs well overall at a 20-day horizon, yet the 2024 holdout coincides with an AI thematic rally and sector rotation that breaks the signal at longer horizons and weakens 20d. This motivates treating deployment as two decisions: (i) whether the strategy should trade at all, and (ii) how to control risk within active trades. We adapt Direct Epistemic Uncertainty Prediction (DEUP) to ranking by predicting rank displacement and defining an epistemic uncertainty signal ehat relative to a point-in-time (PIT-safe) baseline. Empirically, ehat is structurally coupled with signal strength (median correlation between ehat and absolute score is about 0.6 across 1,865 dates), so inverse-uncertainty sizing de-levers the strongest signals and degrades performance. To address this, we propose a two-level deployment policy: a strategy-level regime-trust gate G(t) that decides whether to trade (AUROC around 0.72 overall and 0.75 in FINAL) and a position-level epistemic tail-risk cap that reduces exposure only for the most uncertain predictions. The operational policy, trade only when G(t) is at least 0.2, apply volatility sizing on active dates, and cap the top epistemic tail, improves risk-adjusted performance in the 20d policy comparison and indicates DEUP adds value mainly as a tail-risk guard rather than a continuous sizing denominator.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes uncertainty-aware deployment of stock ranking models using DEUP-derived signals to manage regime shifts and tail risk.
AI.motivation: Traditional cross-sectional rankers are used as if point predictions were reliable, but under non-stationarity and regime shifts (like AI sector rallies) their signals can break, particularly at certain horizons. The authors observe such a failure in their AI Stock Forecaster during a 2024 AI rally, motivating better deployment logic beyond raw scores.
AI.method: Adapt Direct Epistemic Uncertainty Prediction (DEUP) to ranking by predicting rank displacement and defining an epistemic uncertainty signal ehat relative to a point-in-time safe baseline. Analyze coupling between ehat and signal strength, and design a two-level deployment policy: (1) a strategy-level regime-trust gate G(t) to decide when to trade, and (2) a position-level epistemic tail-risk cap to limit exposure on the most uncertain predictions. Evaluate via AUROC for G(t) and risk-adjusted performance for different deployment policies at a 20-day horizon.
AI.result: Empirically, the DEUP-based epistemic uncertainty ehat is strongly correlated with absolute score (median correlation about 0.6 across 1,865 dates), so naive inverse-uncertainty sizing unintentionally de-levers the strongest signals and worsens performance. The proposed two-level policy—with a regime-trust gate achieving AUROC ~0.72 overall (0.75 in FINAL) and an epistemic tail-risk cap—improves risk-adjusted performance in 20-day policy comparisons.
AI.conclusion: DEUP-style epistemic uncertainty is not effective as a smooth, continuous sizing denominator for ranking-based strategies because it is structurally tied to signal strength. Instead, its main practical value is as a regime-level on/off gate and as a tail-risk guard that selectively caps only the most uncertain predictions. A simple operational rule—trade only when regime-trust G(t)≥0.2, then apply volatility sizing and an epistemic tail cap—yields better deployment of stock ranking models under regime shifts.

title: Can We Trust LLMs on Memristors? Diving into Reasoning Ability under Non-Ideality
summary: Memristor-based analog compute-in-memory (CIM) architectures provide a promising substrate for the efficient deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs), owing to superior energy efficiency and computational density. However, these architectures suffer from precision issues caused by intrinsic non-idealities of memristors. In this paper, we first conduct a comprehensive investigation into the impact of such typical non-idealities on LLM reasoning. Empirical results indicate that reasoning capability decreases significantly but varies for distinct benchmarks. Subsequently, we systematically appraise three training-free strategies, including thinking mode, in-context learning, and module redundancy. We thus summarize valuable guidelines, i.e., shallow layer redundancy is particularly effective for improving robustness, thinking mode performs better under low noise levels but degrades at higher noise, and in-context learning reduces output length with a slight performance trade-off. Our findings offer new insights into LLM reasoning under non-ideality and practical strategies to improve robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how analog memristor-based compute-in-memory hardware non-idealities affect LLM reasoning, and evaluates simple, training-free strategies to make LLMs more robust on such hardware.
AI.motivation: While memristor-based analog CIM architectures are attractive for running LLMs efficiently due to their high energy efficiency and computational density, device- and circuit-level non-idealities introduce noise and precision loss, which can significantly harm LLM reasoning performance.
