There is limited understanding of how different non-idealities affect diverse reasoning benchmarks and what practical, training-free methods can mitigate these issues.
AI.method: The authors first model and inject typical memristor non-idealities into LLM inference to analyze their effects on various reasoning benchmarks. They then evaluate three training-free robustness strategies: (1) using different LLM thinking modes (e.g., chain-of-thought style reasoning), (2) leveraging in-context learning with carefully chosen exemplars, and (3) adding redundancy at specific model layers or modules (replicating and aggregating outputs). They systematically compare these methods under different noise levels and configurations.
AI.result: They find that memristor non-idealities significantly degrade reasoning performance, with the severity depending on the specific benchmark. Among mitigation strategies, redundancy in shallow layers is particularly effective at improving robustness, thinking-mode prompting helps under low noise but its benefit diminishes or reverses at higher noise levels, and in-context learning can modestly improve robustness but often shortens generated outputs and entails a small performance trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Non-idealities in memristor-based CIM hardware can strongly impair LLM reasoning, but their impact is task-dependent. Simple, training-free strategies can partially recover performance. Shallow-layer redundancy is the most generally robust technique; reasoning-oriented prompting (thinking mode) is best suited to relatively clean hardware; and in-context learning offers a nuanced trade-off between robustness, output length, and accuracy. These insights provide practical guidance for deploying LLMs on imperfect analog CIM hardware and for future robustness-oriented co-design of models and hardware.

title: Distilling Deep Reinforcement Learning into Interpretable Fuzzy Rules: An Explainable AI Framework
comment: Accepted to AAAI 2026 Spring Symposium Series
summary: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agents achieve remarkable performance in continuous control but remain opaque, hindering deployment in safety-critical domains. Existing explainability methods either provide only local insights (SHAP, LIME) or employ over-simplified surrogates failing to capture continuous dynamics (decision trees). This work proposes a Hierarchical Takagi-Sugeno-Kang (TSK) Fuzzy Classifier System (FCS) distilling neural policies into human-readable IF-THEN rules through K-Means clustering for state partitioning and Ridge Regression for local action inference. Three quantifiable metrics are introduced: Fuzzy Rule Activation Density (FRAD) measuring explanation focus, Fuzzy Set Coverage (FSC) validating vocabulary completeness, and Action Space Granularity (ASG) assessing control mode diversity. Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) validates temporal behavioral fidelity. Empirical evaluation on \textit{Lunar Lander(Continuous)} shows the Triangular membership function variant achieves 81.48\% $\pm$ 0.43\% fidelity, outperforming Decision Trees by 21 percentage points. The framework exhibits statistically superior interpretability (FRAD = 0.814 vs. 0.723 for Gaussian, $p < 0.001$) with low MSE (0.0053) and DTW distance (1.05). Extracted rules such as ``IF lander drifting left at high altitude THEN apply upward thrust with rightward correction'' enable human verification, establishing a pathway toward trustworthy autonomous systems.

AI.tldr: They distill deep RL control policies into interpretable fuzzy IF–THEN rules with a hierarchical TSK fuzzy classifier, introducing new metrics to quantify interpretability and fidelity, and show high-fidelity explanations on LunarLanderContinuous.
AI.motivation: DRL policies are powerful for continuous control but are black-box and hard to trust in safety-critical settings. Existing XAI methods give only local, instance-level explanations or use oversimplified discrete surrogates that cannot express continuous dynamics. The authors want a global, human-readable, and quantitatively evaluable explanation model that still captures the continuous behavior of DRL policies.
AI.method: They train a Hierarchical Takagi–Sugeno–Kang Fuzzy Classifier System as a surrogate for a neural DRL policy. K-means clustering partitions the state space, defining fuzzy regions; Ridge Regression in each region approximates the local action mapping. They define three interpretability metrics—Fuzzy Rule Activation Density (how concentrated rule usage is), Fuzzy Set Coverage (how well the fuzzy partitions cover the state space), and Action Space Granularity (diversity of distinct control modes). They also use Dynamic Time Warping on trajectories to measure temporal similarity between the fuzzy surrogate and the original policy. Variants with different membership functions (e.g., triangular vs Gaussian) are compared.
AI.result: On the LunarLanderContinuous benchmark, the triangular-membership fuzzy model achieves about 81.5% policy fidelity, outperforming decision-tree surrogates by about 21 percentage points. It also attains better interpretability metrics (e.g., higher FRAD) and strong predictive accuracy (low MSE) and trajectory similarity (low DTW distance) relative to alternatives. Concrete natural-language rules are extracted that describe when and how thrust corrections are applied.
AI.conclusion: A hierarchical TSK fuzzy surrogate can faithfully and interpretably approximate DRL policies for continuous control. The proposed metrics quantify both interpretability and behavioral fidelity, and experiments show that appropriately designed fuzzy models (e.g., with triangular memberships) outperform common surrogate baselines like decision trees, offering a promising route toward trustworthy, human-verifiable autonomous control systems.

title: Knowledge Distillation for Large Language Models
comment: Code and data are available at: https://github.com/AlejandroParedesLT/knowledge_distillLLM
summary: We propose a resource-efficient framework for compressing large language models through knowledge distillation, combined with guided chain-of-thought reinforcement learning. Using Qwen 3B as the teacher and Qwen 0.5B as the student, we apply knowledge distillation across English Dolly-15k, Spanish Dolly-15k, and code BugNet and PyTorrent datasets, with hyperparameters tuned in the English setting to optimize student performance. Across tasks, the distilled student retains a substantial portion of the teacher's capability while remaining significantly smaller: 70% to 91% in English, up to 95% in Spanish, and up to 93.5% Rouge-L in code. For coding tasks, integrating chain-of-thought prompting with Group Relative Policy Optimization using CoT-annotated Codeforces data improves reasoning coherence and solution correctness compared to knowledge distillation alone. Post-training 4-bit weight quantization further reduces memory footprint and inference latency. These results show that knowledge distillation combined with chain-of-thought guided reinforcement learning can produce compact, efficient models suitable for deployment in resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: They compress a 3B-parameter LLM into a 0.5B model using knowledge distillation plus chain-of-thought RL, achieving high retention of performance across language and code tasks with additional gains from CoT-guided GRPO and 4-bit quantization.
AI.motivation: Large language models are powerful but too resource-intensive for many deployment environments. There is a need for methods that shrink models substantially while preserving most of their capabilities, especially for multilingual and coding tasks, and that remain compatible with low-precision deployment.
AI.method: Use Qwen 3B as teacher and Qwen 0.5B as student. Perform supervised knowledge distillation on English Dolly-15k, Spanish Dolly-15k, BugNet, and PyTorrent, tuning hyperparameters on English and transferring to other settings. For coding, augment training with chain-of-thought prompting and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using CoT-annotated Codeforces data to improve reasoning. Finally, apply post-training 4-bit weight quantization for further efficiency gains.
AI.result: The 0.5B student retains 70–91% of teacher performance on English tasks, up to 95% on Spanish tasks, and up to 93.5% Rouge-L on code generation compared to the teacher. For coding tasks, adding CoT + GRPO improves reasoning coherence and correctness over pure distillation. 4-bit quantization further decreases memory use and latency with acceptable quality loss.
AI.conclusion: Combining cross-lingual/code knowledge distillation with chain-of-thought-guided GRPO yields compact 0.5B models that preserve most of a 3B teacher’s performance. With additional 4-bit quantization, these models become practical for deployment in resource-constrained environments while maintaining strong language and coding capabilities.

title: Deep Convolutional Architectures for EEG Classification: A Comparative Study with Temporal Augmentation and Confidence-Based Voting
comment: 14 pages, 8 figures, Recent Trends in Image Processing and Pattern Recognition, Copyright held by Springer CCIS
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) classification plays a key role in brain-computer interface (BCI) systems, yet it remains challenging due to the low signal-to-noise ratio, temporal variability of neural responses, and limited data availability. In this paper, we present a comparative study of deep learning architectures for classifying event-related potentials (ERPs) in EEG signals. The preprocessing pipeline includes bandpass filtering, spatial filtering, and normalization. We design and compare three main pipelines: a 2D convolutional neural network (CNN) using Common Spatial Pattern (CSP), a second 2D CNN trained directly on raw data for a fair comparison, and a 3D CNN that jointly models spatiotemporal representations. To address ERP latency variations, we introduce a temporal shift augmentation strategy during training. At inference time, we employ a confidence-based test-time voting mechanism to improve prediction stability across shifted trials. An experimental evaluation on a stratified five-fold cross-validation protocol demonstrates that while CSP provides a benefit to the 2D architecture, the proposed 3D CNN significantly outperforms both 2D variants in terms of AUC and balanced accuracy. These findings highlight the effectiveness of temporal-aware architectures and augmentation strategies for robust EEG signal classification.

AI.tldr: This paper compares deep learning architectures for EEG ERP classification and shows that a 3D spatiotemporal CNN with temporal shift augmentation and confidence-based test-time voting achieves the best performance.
AI.motivation: EEG-based BCI systems rely on accurate classification of ERPs, but this is difficult due to noisy signals, temporal variability of neural responses, and limited training data. The authors aim to find which deep learning architectures and training strategies are most effective for robust ERP classification under these constraints.
AI.method: They apply a preprocessing pipeline with bandpass filtering, spatial filtering, and normalization, then design three architectures: (1) a 2D CNN fed with CSP-transformed signals, (2) a 2D CNN trained directly on raw EEG for a fair baseline, and (3) a 3D CNN that jointly captures spatial and temporal information. They introduce temporal shift data augmentation during training to handle ERP latency variability, and use a confidence-based voting scheme at inference across multiple time-shifted versions of each trial. Performance is evaluated via stratified 5-fold cross-validation using AUC and balanced accuracy.
AI.result: The 3D CNN that jointly models spatiotemporal representations outperforms both 2D CNN variants in terms of AUC and balanced accuracy. CSP improves the 2D CNN compared with training directly on raw data, but is still inferior to the 3D CNN. The temporal shift augmentation and confidence-based test-time voting enhance prediction robustness to latency variations.
AI.conclusion: Temporal-aware deep architectures that directly learn joint spatiotemporal features from EEG, combined with temporal shift augmentation and confidence-based voting, provide more accurate and robust ERP classification than traditional CSP-based 2D CNNs or raw-data 2D CNNs. This suggests that such temporal-aware strategies are promising directions for improving BCI performance.

title: LiveWeb-IE: A Benchmark For Online Web Information Extraction
summary: Web information extraction (WIE) is the task of automatically extracting data from web pages, offering high utility for various applications. The evaluation of WIE systems has traditionally relied on benchmarks built from HTML snapshots captured at a single point in time. However, this offline evaluation paradigm fails to account for the temporally evolving nature of the web; consequently, performance on these static benchmarks often fails to generalize to dynamic real-world scenarios. To bridge this gap, we introduce \dataset, a new benchmark designed for evaluating WIE systems directly against live websites. Based on trusted and permission-granted websites, we curate natural language queries that require information extraction of various data categories, such as text, images, and hyperlinks. We further design these queries to represent four levels of complexity, based on the number and cardinality of attributes to be extracted, enabling a granular assessment of WIE systems. In addition, we propose Visual Grounding Scraper (VGS), a novel multi-stage agentic framework that mimics human cognitive processes by visually narrowing down web page content to extract desired information. Extensive experiments across diverse backbone models demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of VGS. We believe that this study lays the foundation for developing practical and robust WIE systems.

AI.tldr: They introduce a new live-website benchmark and an agentic framework (VGS) for web information extraction that better reflects the dynamic nature of the web than static HTML benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing WIE evaluations use static HTML snapshots taken once, which ignore that websites change over time, causing a mismatch between benchmark performance and real-world performance. There is a need for an evaluation setup and methods that work reliably on live, evolving web pages and across diverse content types.
AI.method: 1) Construct a benchmark (\dataset) using permission-granted, trustworthy live websites and natural language queries that require extracting text, images, and links. 2) Design queries with four graded complexity levels based on the number and cardinality of attributes to extract for fine-grained evaluation. 3) Propose Visual Grounding Scraper (VGS), a multi-stage, agentic pipeline that uses visual grounding to progressively narrow down relevant page regions, mimicking human browsing and extraction behavior. 4) Run extensive experiments using different backbone models within VGS to test its performance and robustness.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple backbone models show that VGS performs effectively and robustly on the new live-website benchmark, handling diverse data types and complexity levels better than prior approaches (implied, though baselines are not detailed in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Evaluating WIE directly on live sites is both feasible and beneficial. The proposed \dataset benchmark plus the VGS framework together provide a stronger, more realistic basis for building and assessing practical, robust web information extraction systems.

title: Multi-hop Reasoning and Retrieval in Embedding Space: Leveraging Large Language Models with Knowledge
summary: As large language models (LLMs) continue to grow in size, their abilities to tackle complex tasks have significantly improved. However, issues such as hallucination and the lack of up-to-date knowledge largely remain unresolved. Knowledge graphs (KGs), which serve as symbolic representations of real-world knowledge, offer a reliable source for enhancing reasoning. Integrating KG retrieval into LLMs can therefore strengthen their reasoning by providing dependable knowledge. Nevertheless, due to limited understanding of the underlying knowledge graph, LLMs may struggle with queries that have multiple interpretations. Additionally, the incompleteness and noise within knowledge graphs may result in retrieval failures. To address these challenges, we propose an embedding-based retrieval reasoning framework EMBRAG. In this approach, the model first generates multiple logical rules grounded in knowledge graphs based on the input query. These rules are then applied to reasoning in the embedding space, guided by the knowledge graph, ensuring more robust and accurate reasoning. A reranker model further interprets these rules and refines the results. Extensive experiments on two benchmark KGQA datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves the new state-of-the-art performance in KG reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EMBRAG, an embedding-based framework that combines logical rules over knowledge graphs with vector-space reasoning to improve LLM-based knowledge graph question answering, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Large language models are powerful but still hallucinate and lack fresh knowledge. Knowledge graphs can provide reliable factual grounding, but LLMs struggle with ambiguous, multi-interpretation queries and with incomplete or noisy graphs that cause retrieval failures. The authors are motivated to design a method that uses KGs more robustly by combining symbolic structure with embedding-based reasoning so that queries can be interpreted and answered accurately even when the graph is imperfect.
AI.method: They propose EMBRAG, an embedding-based retrieval reasoning framework. Given an input query, the model first generates multiple logical rules that are explicitly grounded in the knowledge graph. These rules act as interpretable reasoning templates. Instead of operating only on discrete graph structure, EMBRAG performs reasoning in the embedding space, guided by these rules and the KG topology, which allows it to be more tolerant of KG incompleteness and noise. A reranker model then evaluates and interprets the candidate rules and their results, selecting and refining the best answers. The entire pipeline thus combines rule generation, embedding-space rule application, and reranking for KG question answering.
AI.result: On two benchmark knowledge graph question answering datasets, EMBRAG achieves new state-of-the-art performance on KG reasoning metrics, outperforming prior methods that either rely purely on symbolic KG reasoning or on LLM-based approaches without this embedding-guided rule mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Embedding-based reasoning guided by logical rules over knowledge graphs can significantly improve robustness and accuracy in KG question answering with LLMs, particularly under query ambiguity and KG incompleteness. The proposed EMBRAG framework demonstrates that integrating rule generation, embedding-space reasoning, and reranking yields superior performance compared to existing KG reasoning approaches.

title: Generate Then Correct: Single Shot Global Correction for Aspect Sentiment Quad Prediction
summary: Aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) extracts aspect-level sentiment signals from user-generated text, supports product analytics, experience monitoring, and public-opinion tracking, and is central to fine-grained opinion mining. A key challenge in ABSA is aspect sentiment quad prediction (ASQP), which requires identifying four elements: the aspect term, the aspect category, the opinion term, and the sentiment polarity. However, existing studies usually linearize the unordered quad set into a fixed-order template and decode it left-to-right. With teacher forcing training, the resulting training-inference mismatch (exposure bias) lets early prefix errors propagate to later elements. The linearization order determines which elements appear earlier in the prefix, so this propagation becomes order-sensitive and is hard to repair in a single pass. To address this, we propose a method, Generate-then-Correct (G2C): a generator drafts quads and a corrector performs a single-shot, sequence-level global correction trained on LLM-synthesized drafts with common error patterns. On the Rest15 and Rest16 datasets, G2C outperforms strong baseline models.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Generate-then-Correct (G2C) framework for aspect sentiment quad prediction (ASQP) to reduce exposure bias and improve performance.
AI.motivation: Aspect-based sentiment analysis at the quad level suffers from exposure bias because existing methods linearize unordered quads into fixed-order sequences and decode them left-to-right with teacher forcing, causing early errors to propagate and making performance sensitive to the chosen order.
AI.method: Introduce a two-stage G2C framework where a generator first drafts aspect sentiment quads and a corrector then applies single-shot, sequence-level global correction. The corrector is trained using LLM-synthesized draft errors that mimic common error patterns, enabling it to fix generator mistakes regardless of quad linearization order.
AI.result: On benchmark restaurant review datasets Rest15 and Rest16, the G2C framework achieves better performance than strong baseline models for aspect sentiment quad prediction.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling quad generation from correction and training a global corrector on synthetic error patterns effectively mitigates exposure bias and order sensitivity in ASQP, leading to state-of-the-art or improved performance on standard ABSA benchmarks.

title: Agent-Based User-Adaptive Filtering for Categorized Harassing Communication
comment: 10 pages, 59 figures. Revised and archived version of ALA 2019 workshop paper (co-located with AAMAS 2019)
summary: We propose an agent-based framework for personalized filtering of categorized harassing communication in online social networks. Unlike global moderation systems that apply uniform filtering rules, our approach models user-specific tolerance levels and preferences through adaptive filtering agents. These agents learn from user feedback and dynamically adjust filtering thresholds across multiple harassment categories, including offensive, abusive, and hateful content. We implement and evaluate the framework using supervised classification techniques and simulated user interaction data. Experimental results demonstrate that adaptive agents improve filtering precision and user satisfaction compared to static models. The proposed system illustrates how agent-based personalization can enhance content moderation while preserving user autonomy in digital social environments.

AI.tldr: An agent-based, personalized content filtering framework for online social networks that adapts to each user’s tolerance and preferences for different harassment categories, improving precision and satisfaction over static filters.
AI.motivation: Global, one-size-fits-all moderation rules fail to reflect individual users’ differing tolerance levels and preferences regarding harassing content, which can either over-censor or under-protect users. The paper aims to design a moderation system that personalizes filtering while maintaining user autonomy and better addressing diverse needs in online social networks.
AI.method: The authors design adaptive filtering agents assigned to individual users. These agents model user-specific tolerance thresholds for multiple harassment categories (offensive, abusive, hateful). Using supervised text classification as the base detector and simulated user interaction/feedback as training signals, the agents iteratively update category-specific thresholds and filtering rules. They then compare this adaptive approach against static, non-personalized filtering models.
AI.result: Experiments using simulated user feedback and supervised classifiers show that the adaptive agent-based system achieves higher filtering precision than static models and leads to higher measured user satisfaction in the simulations. The system more accurately matches each user’s desired level of content blocking across harassment categories.
AI.conclusion: Agent-based personalization of content filtering can make online moderation more accurate and user-centric. By learning individual tolerance profiles and dynamically adjusting thresholds, the framework improves filtering precision and user satisfaction relative to static approaches, suggesting a promising direction for moderation systems that preserve user autonomy while mitigating exposure to harassing content.

title: Projection-Free Evolution Strategies for Continuous Prompt Search
summary: Continuous prompt search offers a computationally efficient alternative to conventional parameter tuning in natural language processing tasks. Nevertheless, its practical effectiveness can be significantly hindered by the black-box nature and the inherent high-dimensionality of the objective landscapes. Existing methods typically mitigate these challenges by restricting the search to a randomly projected low-dimensional subspace. However, the effectiveness and underlying motivation of the projection mechanism remain ambiguous. In this paper, we first empirically demonstrate that despite the prompt space possessing a low-dimensional structure, random projections fail to adequately capture this essential structure. Motivated by this finding, we propose a projection-free prompt search method based on evolutionary strategies. By directly optimizing in the full prompt space with an adaptation mechanism calibrated to the intrinsic dimension, our method achieves competitive search capabilities without additional computational overhead. Furthermore, to bridge the generalization gap in few-shot scenarios, we introduce a confidence-based regularization mechanism that systematically enhances the model's confidence in the target verbalizers. Experimental results on seven natural language understanding tasks from the GLUE benchmark demonstrate that our proposed approach significantly outperforms existing baselines.

AI.tldr: They propose a projection-free evolutionary strategy method for continuous prompt search that adapts to intrinsic dimension and adds confidence-based regularization, outperforming prior projection-based methods on GLUE tasks.
AI.motivation: Continuous prompt search is efficient but hampered by black-box, high-dimensional landscapes. Existing random low-dimensional projections are ad-hoc and may not capture the true low-dimensional structure of prompt space; the authors want to understand and improve this.
AI.method: (1) Empirically analyze prompt space structure and show random projections inadequately capture it. (2) Design an evolutionary-strategy-based optimization that operates directly in the full prompt space, with step-size/adaptation calibrated to the intrinsic dimension instead of using explicit projections. (3) Add a confidence-based regularization term that encourages the model to assign higher confidence to target verbalizers, especially in few-shot settings.
AI.result: Their projection-free ES-based prompt search achieves competitive or better search performance than projection-based methods at no extra computational cost. With confidence-based regularization, it significantly improves few-shot generalization. On seven GLUE NLU tasks, it consistently and significantly outperforms existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Random low-dimensional projections are not an effective way to exploit the intrinsic low-dimensional structure of prompt space. A projection-free, intrinsically-calibrated evolutionary search combined with confidence-based regularization yields better continuous prompt tuning, leading to state-of-the-art or superior results on GLUE with similar compute costs.

title: DOVA: Deliberation-First Multi-Agent Orchestration for Autonomous Research Automation
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in tool use, reasoning, and code generation, yet single-agent systems exhibit fundamental limitations when confronted with complex research tasks demanding multi-source synthesis, adversarial verification, and personalized delivery. We present DOVA (Deep Orchestrated Versatile Agent), a multi-agent platform introducing three key innovations: (1) deliberation-first orchestration, where explicit meta-reasoning precedes tool invocation, informed by a persistent user model and entity-aware conversation context; (2) hybrid collaborative reasoning, a composable three-phase pipeline unifying ensemble diversity, blackboard transparency, and iterative refinement; and (3) adaptive multi-tiered thinking, a six-level token-budget allocation scheme that reduces inference cost by 40-60% on simple tasks while preserving deep reasoning capacity. We formalize the core algorithms, present an architectural ablation study across seven system configurations, and analyze the contribution of each component to answer confidence, source coverage, and token efficiency.

AI.tldr: DOVA is a multi-agent LLM platform that orchestrates multiple specialized agents with meta-reasoning and adaptive thinking to improve complex research assistance while cutting token costs.
AI.motivation: Single LLM agents struggle with complex research tasks that require combining multiple sources, verifying information adversarially, and tailoring responses to users. There is a need for a systematic multi-agent architecture that improves reliability, transparency, and efficiency instead of ad-hoc tool-calling chains.
AI.method: The paper proposes DOVA, a multi-agent framework with three main ideas: (1) deliberation-first orchestration that performs meta-reasoning and planning before calling tools, guided by a persistent user model and entity-aware context; (2) a hybrid collaborative reasoning pipeline with three phases that combine diverse agent ensembles, a shared blackboard for transparent state, and iterative refinement; and (3) an adaptive, multi-tier thinking mechanism that allocates different token budgets across six levels of reasoning complexity to balance cost and depth. They formalize the algorithms and run ablations over seven system variants.
AI.result: DOVA achieves 40-60% reduction in inference token cost on simpler tasks while maintaining deep reasoning for harder problems. Ablation studies show how each architectural component contributes to improvements in answer confidence, breadth of source coverage, and token efficiency across configurations.
AI.conclusion: A carefully orchestrated multi-agent LLM system with explicit meta-reasoning, collaborative pipelines, and adaptive thinking can outperform single-agent baselines in complex research assistance, yielding more reliable, source-rich answers at significantly lower token cost.

title: DeceptGuard :A Constitutional Oversight Framework For Detecting Deception in LLM Agents
summary:
Reliable detection of deceptive behavior in Large Language Model (LLM) agents is an essential prerequisite for safe deployment in high-stakes agentic contexts. Prior work on scheming detection has focused exclusively on black-box monitors that observe only externally visible tool calls and outputs, discarding potentially rich internal reasoning signals. We introduce DECEPTGUARD, a unified framework that systematically compares three monitoring regimes: black-box monitors (actions and outputs only), CoT-aware monitors (additionally observing the agent's chain-of-thought reasoning trace), and activation-probe monitors (additionally reading hidden-state representations from a frozen open-weights encoder).
  We introduce DECEPTSYNTH, a scalable synthetic pipeline for generating deception-positive and deception-negative agent trajectories across a novel 12-category taxonomy spanning verbal, behavioral, and structural deception. Our monitors are optimized on 4,800 synthetic trajectories and evaluated on 9,200 held-out samples from DeceptArena, a benchmark of realistic sandboxed agent environments with execution-verified labels.
  Across all evaluation settings, CoT-aware and activation-probe monitors substantially outperform their black-box counterparts (mean pAUROC improvement of +0.097), with the largest gains on subtle, long-horizon deception that leaves minimal behavioral footprints. We empirically characterize a transparency-detectability trade-off: as agents learn to suppress overt behavioral signals, chain-of-thought becomes the primary detection surface but is itself increasingly unreliable due to post-training faithfulness degradation.
  We propose HYBRID-CONSTITUTIONAL ensembles as a robust defense-in-depth approach, achieving a pAUROC of 0.934 on the held-out test set, representing a substantial advance over the prior state of the art.

AI.tldr: The paper presents DECEPTGUARD, a framework for detecting deceptive behavior in LLM agents using different monitoring regimes, and shows that incorporating internal reasoning and activations significantly improves detection performance over black-box approaches.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents are increasingly used in high-stakes, autonomous settings, it becomes critical to reliably detect when they behave deceptively. Existing scheming detection methods mostly rely on black-box monitoring of outputs and tool calls, ignoring potentially informative internal reasoning traces and model activations. There is a need for systematic comparison of different monitoring levels and for realistic benchmarks of deceptive behavior to guide safety interventions.
AI.method: The authors introduce DECEPTGUARD, which compares three monitoring regimes: (1) black-box monitors that see only actions and outputs, (2) CoT-aware monitors that also observe chain-of-thought traces, and (3) activation-probe monitors that additionally access hidden-state representations from a frozen open-weights encoder. They build DECEPTSYNTH, a synthetic data pipeline generating deception-positive and deception-negative trajectories across a 12-category deception taxonomy (verbal, behavioral, structural). Monitors are trained/optimized on 4,800 synthetic trajectories and then evaluated on 9,200 held-out samples from DeceptArena, a benchmark of realistic sandboxed agent environments with execution-verified deception labels. They also explore ensemble methods, specifically HYBRID-CONSTITUTIONAL ensembles.
AI.result: CoT-aware and activation-probe monitors achieve substantially better performance than black-box monitors, with a mean partial AUROC improvement of +0.097 across evaluation settings. The largest gains occur for subtle, long-horizon deception with minimal behavioral traces. The authors identify a transparency-detectability trade-off: when agents suppress overt behavioral signals, chain-of-thought becomes the main detection surface but grows less reliable due to declining faithfulness after further training. Their HYBRID-CONSTITUTIONAL ensemble achieves a pAUROC of 0.934 on the held-out DeceptArena test set, significantly outperforming prior state-of-the-art deception detection methods.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating internal reasoning traces and model activations into monitoring frameworks can markedly improve the detection of deceptive behavior in LLM agents, particularly for subtle forms of deception. However, there is an inherent trade-off between making agents more transparent and the reliability of those transparency signals as agents adapt. HYBRID-CONSTITUTIONAL ensemble monitoring offers a strong defense-in-depth strategy, setting a new performance benchmark on DeceptArena and highlighting the value of multi-surface monitoring for LLM safety.

title: Why Grokking Takes So Long: A First-Principles Theory of Representational Phase Transitions
summary:
Grokking is the sudden generalization that appears long after a model has perfectly memorized its training data. Although this phenomenon has been widely observed, there is still no quantitative theory explaining the length of the delay between memorization and generalization. Prior work has noted that weight decay plays an important role, but no result derives tight bounds for the delay or explains its scaling behavior.
  We present a first-principles theory showing that grokking arises from a norm-driven representational phase transition in regularized training dynamics. Training first converges to a high-norm memorization solution and only later contracts toward a lower-norm structured representation that generalizes.
  Our main result establishes a scaling law for the delay:
  T_grok - T_mem = Theta((1 / gamma_eff) * log(||theta_mem||^2 / ||theta_post||^2)),
  where gamma_eff is the effective contraction rate of the optimizer (gamma_eff = eta * lambda for SGD and gamma_eff >= eta * lambda for AdamW). The upper bound follows from a discrete Lyapunov contraction argument, and the matching lower bound arises from dynamical constraints of regularized first-order optimization.
  Across 293 training runs spanning modular addition, modular multiplication, and sparse parity tasks, we confirm three predictions: inverse scaling with weight decay, inverse scaling with learning rate, and logarithmic dependence on the norm ratio (R^2 > 0.97). We further find that grokking requires an optimizer that can decouple memorization from contraction: SGD fails under hyperparameters where AdamW reliably groks.
  These results show that grokking is a predictable consequence of norm separation between competing interpolating representations and provide the first quantitative scaling law for the delay of grokking.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a quantitative theory of grokking, linking the delay between memorization and generalization to a norm-driven phase transition under weight decay.
AI.motivation: Grokking—very late generalization after perfect memorization—has been empirically observed but lacks a quantitative theory explaining how long the delay between memorization and generalization should be and how it scales with training hyperparameters like weight decay and learning rate.
AI.method: The authors model training with weight decay as a regularized dynamical system, analyze its trajectories via a norm-driven representational phase transition between high-norm memorizing and low-norm generalizing solutions, and use Lyapunov-style contraction arguments to derive tight upper and lower bounds on the grokking delay for first-order optimizers such as SGD and AdamW. They then empirically validate the theory on modular addition, modular multiplication, and sparse parity across 293 runs.
AI.result: They derive a scaling law for the grokking delay: T_grok - T_mem = Θ((1 / γ_eff) * log(||θ_mem||^2 / ||θ_post||^2)), where γ_eff is the effective contraction rate depending on learning rate and weight decay. Experiments confirm inverse scaling with weight decay, inverse scaling with learning rate, and logarithmic dependence on the ratio of memorizing to post-grokking parameter norms, with R^2 > 0.97. They also show that AdamW can grok in regimes where SGD fails because AdamW better decouples memorization dynamics from norm contraction.
AI.conclusion: Grokking is explained as a predictable, norm-driven representational phase transition between memorizing and structured generalizing solutions under regularized first-order optimization. The paper provides the first quantitative scaling law for grokking delay, clarifies the role of optimizer and weight decay, and shows that norm separation between competing interpolating solutions is the key mechanism behind grokking.

title: GhanaNLP Parallel Corpora: Comprehensive Multilingual Resources for Low-Resource Ghanaian Languages
summary: Low resource languages present unique challenges for natural language processing due to the limited availability of digitized and well structured linguistic data. To address this gap, the GhanaNLP initiative has developed and curated 41,513 parallel sentence pairs for the Twi, Fante, Ewe, Ga, and Kusaal languages, which are widely spoken across Ghana yet remain underrepresented in digital spaces. Each dataset consists of carefully aligned sentence pairs between a local language and English. The data were collected, translated, and annotated by human professionals and enriched with standard structural metadata to ensure consistency and usability. These corpora are designed to support research, educational, and commercial applications, including machine translation, speech technologies, and language preservation. This paper documents the dataset creation methodology, structure, intended use cases, and evaluation, as well as their deployment in real world applications such as the Khaya AI translation engine. Overall, this work contributes to broader efforts to democratize AI by enabling inclusive and accessible language technologies for African languages.

AI.tldr: They built and released a high quality parallel corpus (41,513 sentence pairs) for five low resource Ghanaian languages paired with English to support NLP applications.
AI.motivation: Low resource Ghanaian languages like Twi, Fante, Ewe, Ga, and Kusaal lack digitized, well structured data, which hinders the development of NLP tools and inclusive AI technologies for these widely spoken yet underrepresented languages.
AI.method: The GhanaNLP initiative collected, translated, and annotated sentence pairs between each local language and English using human professionals, then carefully aligned them and enriched the data with standard structural metadata, documenting the dataset creation process, structure, intended use, and evaluation, and demonstrating deployment in real world systems.
AI.result: They produced five parallel corpora totaling 41,513 high quality sentence pairs, each aligned between a Ghanaian language and English, complete with structural metadata and demonstrated usability in real world applications like the Khaya AI translation engine.
AI.conclusion: The datasets provide crucial foundational resources that enable machine translation, speech technologies, and language preservation for underrepresented Ghanaian languages, contributing to democratizing AI and making language technologies more inclusive and accessible.

title: DyACE: Dynamic Algorithm Co-evolution for Online Automated Heuristic Design with Large Language Model
summary: The prevailing paradigm in Automated Heuristic Design (AHD) typically relies on the assumption that a single, fixed algorithm can effectively navigate the shifting dynamics of a combinatorial search. This static approach often proves inadequate for Perturbative Heuristics, where the optimal algorithm for escaping local optima depends heavily on the specific search phase. To address this limitation, we reformulate heuristic design as a Non-stationary Bi-level Control problem and introduce DyACE (Dynamic Algorithm Co-evolution). Distinct from standard open-loop solvers, DyACE use a Receding Horizon Control architecture to continuously co-evolve the heuristic logic alongside the solution population. A core element of this framework is the Look-Ahead Rollout Search, which queries the landscape geometry to extract Search Trajectory Features. This sensory feedback allows the Large Language Model (LLM) to function as a grounded meta-controller, prescribing phase-specific interventions tailored to the real-time search status. We validate DyACE on three representative combinatorial optimization benchmarks. The results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art static baselines, exhibiting superior scalability in high-dimensional search spaces. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm that dynamic adaptation fails without grounded perception, often performing worse than static algorithms. This indicates that DyACE's effectiveness stems from the causal alignment between the synthesized logic and the verified gradients of the optimization landscape.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DyACE, a dynamic algorithm co-evolution framework for automated heuristic design that adapts perturbative heuristics to different search phases, leading to better performance than static baselines on combinatorial optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing Automated Heuristic Design methods assume a single static algorithm can handle the entire combinatorial search, which is inadequate for perturbative heuristics where the best strategy for escaping local optima varies across search phases. There is a need for a framework that can dynamically adapt heuristic logic based on the current search state.
AI.method: The authors reformulate heuristic design as a Non-stationary Bi-level Control problem and present DyACE, which uses a Receding Horizon Control architecture to co-evolve heuristic logic and solution population. They introduce Look-Ahead Rollout Search to probe the landscape and extract Search Trajectory Features, which serve as grounded sensory feedback to a Large Language Model acting as a meta-controller that prescribes phase-specific heuristic interventions in real time.
AI.result: On three combinatorial optimization benchmarks, DyACE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art static baselines, particularly in high-dimensional search spaces. Ablation studies show that without grounded perception (i.e., without search trajectory features from the rollout), dynamic adaptation underperforms and can even do worse than static algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, perception-grounded adaptation of perturbative heuristics via DyACE yields superior performance and scalability compared to static heuristic designs. The effectiveness of DyACE arises from the causal alignment between the dynamically synthesized heuristic logic and the actual gradients/structure of the optimization landscape, highlighting the importance of grounded feedback for non-stationary control in heuristic search.

title: PMIScore: An Unsupervised Approach to Quantify Dialogue Engagement
comment: 23 pages, 4 figures. Accepted to The Web Conference 2026
summary: High dialogue engagement is a crucial indicator of an effective conversation. A reliable measure of engagement could help benchmark large language models, enhance the effectiveness of human-computer interactions, or improve personal communication skills. However, quantifying engagement is challenging, since it is subjective and lacks a "gold standard". This paper proposes PMIScore, an efficient unsupervised approach to quantify dialogue engagement. It uses pointwise mutual information (PMI), which is the probability of generating a response conditioning on the conversation history. Thus, PMIScore offers a clear interpretation of engagement. As directly computing PMI is intractable due to the complexity of dialogues, PMIScore learned it through a dual form of divergence. The algorithm includes generating positive and negative dialogue pairs, extracting embeddings by large language models (LLMs), and training a small neural network using a mutual information loss function. We validated PMIScore on both synthetic and real-world datasets. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of PMIScore in PMI estimation and the reasonableness of the PMI metric itself.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PMIScore, an unsupervised metric for quantifying dialogue engagement using pointwise mutual information (PMI) estimated via a neural model.
AI.motivation: Dialogue engagement is important for evaluating conversations and LLMs but is hard to measure due to its subjectivity and lack of a gold-standard quantitative metric. The authors want a principled, interpretable, and scalable way to measure engagement.
AI.method: They define engagement in terms of PMI between the conversation history and the next response. Because exact PMI is intractable, they estimate it via a dual-form divergence objective: they (1) construct positive and negative dialogue pairs, (2) encode them using embeddings from large language models, and (3) train a small neural network with a mutual-information-based loss to approximate PMI, producing PMIScore.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world dialogue datasets show that PMIScore can approximate PMI effectively and that higher scores correspond to more engaged dialogues, supporting both the accuracy of their estimator and the usefulness of PMI as an engagement metric.
AI.conclusion: PMIScore is a practical, interpretable, and unsupervised approach for measuring dialogue engagement. It leverages LLM embeddings and an MI-based training objective to approximate PMI and is validated empirically as both a good estimator and a meaningful engagement metric.

title: AutoTool: Automatic Scaling of Tool-Use Capabilities in RL via Decoupled Entropy Constraints
summary: Tool use represents a critical capability for AI agents, with recent advances focusing on leveraging reinforcement learning (RL) to scale up the explicit reasoning process to achieve better performance. However, there are some key challenges for tool use in current RL-based scaling approaches: (a) direct RL training often struggles to scale up thinking length sufficiently to solve complex problems, and (b) scaled-up models tend to overthink simpler problems, resulting in substantial token inefficiency. To address these challenges, we propose a novel training paradigm that first employs warm-up supervised fine-tuning to help models distinguish between simple and complex problems, followed by RL that enable models to automatically determine appropriate reasoning trajectories. Furthermore, to tackle the issue of automatic thinking-length scaling, we discover that entropy-based optimization objectives effectively maintain model diversity while successfully unlocking the model's scaling capabilities. Based on this insight, we introduce an entropy-based long-short reasoning fusion RL strategy. Our experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that model successfully achieves auto-scaling for efficient tool use, achieving significant 9.8\% accuracy improvements while reducing computational overhead by \textasciitilde81\%.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new RL-based training paradigm that enables AI models to automatically adapt their reasoning length for tool use, improving both accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based tool-use methods struggle to sufficiently scale reasoning depth for complex problems and tend to waste computation by overthinking simple problems. There is a need for a training strategy that can both distinguish problem difficulty and automatically adjust reasoning length for efficient tool use.
AI.method: The authors introduce a two-stage training pipeline: (1) a warm-up supervised fine-tuning phase that trains the model to differentiate between simple and complex tasks; (2) a reinforcement learning phase with entropy-based optimization objectives, where they propose an entropy-based long-short reasoning fusion RL strategy that encourages diverse reasoning trajectories and allows the model to select appropriate reasoning lengths for different tasks.
AI.result: On three benchmarks, the proposed approach achieves automatic scaling of reasoning length for tool use, yielding a 9.8% accuracy improvement while reducing computational overhead by approximately 81% compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed entropy-based, two-stage training paradigm effectively enables AI models to perform adaptive reasoning for tool use, balancing performance and efficiency by scaling up thinking for complex tasks while avoiding unnecessary computation on simple ones.

title: APEX-Searcher: Augmenting LLMs' Search Capabilities through Agentic Planning and Execution
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), based on large language models (LLMs), serves as a vital approach to retrieving and leveraging external knowledge in various domain applications. When confronted with complex multi-hop questions, single-round retrieval is often insufficient for accurate reasoning and problem solving. To enhance search capabilities for complex tasks, most existing works integrate multi-round iterative retrieval with reasoning processes via end-to-end training. While these approaches significantly improve problem-solving performance, they are still faced with challenges in task reasoning and model training, especially ambiguous retrieval execution paths and sparse rewards in end-to-end reinforcement learning (RL) process, leading to inaccurate retrieval results and performance degradation. To address these issues, in this paper, we proposes APEX-Searcher, a novel Agentic Planning and Execution framework to augment LLM search capabilities. Specifically, we introduce a two-stage agentic framework that decouples the retrieval process into planning and execution: It first employs RL with decomposition-specific rewards to optimize strategic planning; Built on the sub-task decomposition, it then applies supervised fine-tuning on high-quality multi-hop trajectories to equip the model with robust iterative sub-task execution capabilities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed framework achieves significant improvements in both multi-hop RAG and task planning performances across multiple benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces APEX-Searcher, a two-stage agentic planning-and-execution framework that improves multi-hop retrieval-augmented generation by separating planning and execution and training them with RL and supervised learning respectively.
AI.motivation: Existing RAG systems struggle with complex multi-hop questions because single-round retrieval is insufficient, and current multi-round methods suffer from ambiguous retrieval paths and sparse rewards in end-to-end RL, causing inaccurate retrieval and degraded performance. There is a need for a more structured, trainable search mechanism that can better plan and execute multi-step retrieval for complex reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose APEX-Searcher, which decouples retrieval into two stages: (1) a planning stage trained with reinforcement learning using decomposition-specific rewards to learn how to strategically decompose complex questions into sub-tasks; and (2) an execution stage trained with supervised fine-tuning on high-quality multi-hop trajectories to perform robust iterative sub-task execution based on the generated plans.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple benchmarks show that APEX-Searcher significantly improves performance in both multi-hop retrieval-augmented generation tasks and task planning quality, compared to existing baselines that use single-round or end-to-end multi-round retrieval methods.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling multi-hop retrieval into an RL-optimized planning phase and an SFT-trained execution phase effectively addresses issues of ambiguous retrieval paths and sparse RL rewards in existing RAG approaches, leading to more accurate retrieval, stronger reasoning, and better overall performance on complex multi-hop tasks.

title: Prompt Complexity Dilutes Structured Reasoning: A Follow-Up Study on the Car Wash Problem
summary:
In a previous study [Jo, 2026], STAR reasoning (Situation, Task, Action, Result) raised car wash problem accuracy from 0% to 85% on Claude Sonnet 4.5, and to 100% with
  additional prompt layers. This follow-up asks: does STAR maintain its effectiveness in a production system prompt?
  We tested STAR inside InterviewMate's 60+ line production prompt, which had evolved through iterative additions of style guidelines, format instructions, and profile
  features. Three conditions, 20 trials each, on Claude Sonnet 4.6: (A) production prompt with Anthropic profile, (B) production prompt with default profile, (C) original
  STAR-only prompt. C scored 100% (verified at n=100). A and B scored 0% and 30%.
  Prompt complexity dilutes structured reasoning. STAR achieves 100% in isolation but degrades to 0-30% when surrounded by competing instructions. The mechanism: directives
  like "Lead with specifics" force conclusion-first output, reversing the reason-then-conclude order that makes STAR effective. In one case, the model output "Short answer:
  Walk." then executed STAR reasoning that correctly identified the constraint -- proving the model could reason correctly but had already committed to the wrong answer.
  Cross-model comparison shows STAR-only improved from 85% (Sonnet 4.5) to 100% (Sonnet 4.6) without prompt changes, suggesting model upgrades amplify structured reasoning
  in isolation.
  These results imply structured reasoning frameworks should not be assumed to transfer from isolated testing to complex prompt environments. The order in which a model
  reasons and concludes is a first-class design variable.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether the STAR (Situation, Task, Action, Result) reasoning framework stays effective when embedded in a long, real-world production prompt instead of an isolated test prompt.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed STAR reasoning dramatically improved a model’s accuracy on the car wash problem when used in a clean, standalone prompt. However, real production prompts are long, messy, and full of competing instructions, so it was unclear whether STAR would still work in that setting.
AI.method: The authors embedded STAR into InterviewMate’s 60+ line production system prompt, which also contained style rules, formatting constraints, and profile instructions. They ran three conditions on Claude Sonnet 4.6 with 20 trials each: (A) production prompt with Anthropic profile, (B) production prompt with default profile, and (C) the original STAR-only prompt. Condition C was further verified at n=100.
AI.result: The STAR-only prompt (C) achieved 100% accuracy (confirmed across 100 trials). In contrast, the production prompts showed large performance drops: condition A scored 0% and condition B scored 30%. Analysis of outputs showed that instructions like “Lead with specifics” caused the model to give a conclusion first and then reason, which broke STAR’s reason-then-conclude pattern, even when the internal reasoning was correct.
AI.conclusion: Structured reasoning frameworks like STAR can perform perfectly in isolation but degrade sharply when embedded in complex production prompts with many overlapping instructions. Prompt complexity and output-order directives can override or distort structured reasoning. Therefore, practitioners should not assume lab-style prompt results will transfer to production; the sequencing of reasoning vs. conclusion in the prompt should be treated as a core design decision.

title: GradMem: Learning to Write Context into Memory with Test-Time Gradient Descent
summary: Many large language model applications require conditioning on long contexts. Transformers typically support this by storing a large per-layer KV-cache of past activations, which incurs substantial memory overhead. A desirable alternative is ompressive memory: read a context once, store it in a compact state, and answer many queries from that state. We study this in a context removal setting, where the model must generate an answer without access to the original context at inference time. We introduce GradMem, which writes context into memory via per-sample test-time optimization. Given a context, GradMem performs a few steps of gradient descent on a small set of prefix memory tokens while keeping model weights frozen. GradMem explicitly optimizes a model-level self-supervised context reconstruction loss, resulting in a loss-driven write operation with iterative error correction, unlike forward-only methods. On associative key--value retrieval, GradMem outperforms forward-only memory writers with the same memory size, and additional gradient steps scale capacity much more effectively than repeated forward writes. We further show that GradMem transfers beyond synthetic benchmarks: with pretrained language models, it attains competitive results on natural language tasks including bAbI and SQuAD variants, relying only on information encoded in memory.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes GradMem, a gradient-based compressive memory mechanism for large language models that enables answering queries from a compact memory state without retaining the full context.
AI.motivation: Traditional transformer KV-caches for long-context conditioning are memory-intensive because they store all past activations per layer. There is a need for a more memory-efficient mechanism that can read long contexts once, compress them into a small state, and later answer multiple queries using only this compact memory, even when the original context is unavailable at inference.
AI.method: GradMem introduces a set of learnable prefix memory tokens that are optimized at test time via a few steps of gradient descent, while keeping the model weights frozen. Given a context, the method performs per-sample optimization on these memory tokens to minimize a self-supervised context reconstruction loss, effectively writing the context into memory through an iterative, loss-driven process rather than a single forward pass.
AI.result: On associative key–value retrieval tasks, GradMem achieves better performance than forward-only memory writing methods under the same memory budget. Increasing the number of gradient descent steps significantly improves memory capacity more efficiently than simply repeating forward writes. On real-world language tasks such as bAbI and SQuAD variants, GradMem, when used with pretrained language models, obtains competitive performance relying solely on the information encoded in its compressed memory.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-based test-time optimization of compact memory tokens is an effective alternative to large KV-caches for long-context handling. GradMem’s loss-driven, iterative memory writing leads to higher capacity and competitive performance on both synthetic retrieval benchmarks and natural language tasks, while operating without access to the original context during inference.

title: Optimizing LLM Annotation of Classroom Discourse through Multi-Agent Orchestration
comment: Accepted for presentation at the Education Data Science Conference (EDS 2026), Stanford, USA, May 26-28, 2026. Extended abstract
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly positioned as scalable tools for annotating educational data, including classroom discourse, interaction logs, and qualitative learning artifacts. Their ability to rapidly summarize instructional interactions and assign rubric-aligned labels has fueled optimism about reducing the cost and time associated with expert human annotation. However, growing evidence suggests that single-pass LLM outputs remain unreliable for high-stakes educational constructs that require contextual, pedagogical, or normative judgment, such as instructional intent or discourse moves. This tension between scale and validity sits at the core of contemporary education data science. In this work, we present and empirically evaluate a hierarchical, cost-aware orchestration framework for LLM-based annotation that improves reliability while explicitly modeling computational tradeoffs. Rather than treating annotation as a one-shot prediction problem, we conceptualize it as a multi-stage epistemic process comprising (1) an unverified single-pass annotation stage, in which models independently assign labels based on the rubric; (2) a self-verification stage, in which each model audits its own output against rubric definitions and revises its label if inconsistencies are detected; and (3) a disagreement-centric adjudication stage, in which an independent adjudicator model examines the verified labels and justifications and determines a final label in accordance with the rubric. This structure mirrors established human annotation workflows in educational research, where initial coding is followed by self-checking and expert resolution of disagreements.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hierarchical, multi-stage framework that uses LLMs to annotate educational data more reliably by mimicking human coding workflows and explicitly considering computational cost–accuracy tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: LLMs are promising for scaling annotation of educational data (e.g., classroom discourse, learning artifacts) because they can generate rubric-aligned labels quickly, potentially reducing the cost and time of expert human coding. However, single-pass LLM annotations are often unreliable for complex, high-stakes educational constructs that require contextual, pedagogical, or normative judgment, such as instructional intent or discourse moves. The field faces a core tension between the desire for scalable annotation and the need for validity and reliability in these sophisticated constructs. This motivates designing an LLM orchestration approach that improves reliability while controlling computational cost.
AI.method: The paper conceptualizes annotation as a multi-stage epistemic process and implements a hierarchical, cost-aware orchestration framework for LLM-based annotation: (1) Unverified single-pass annotation: multiple LLM instances independently assign labels to educational data according to a rubric. (2) Self-verification: each model audits its own initial label against the rubric definitions, checks for inconsistencies or rubric violations, and may revise its label accordingly. (3) Disagreement-centric adjudication: when models disagree or uncertainty remains, an independent adjudicator LLM examines the verified labels and justifications, then decides on a final label that best matches the rubric. The framework explicitly models computational tradeoffs (e.g., when to trigger self-verification or adjudication) and is empirically evaluated on educational annotation tasks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that the hierarchical multi-stage orchestration improves reliability of LLM-based annotations on complex educational constructs compared to single-pass LLM predictions. The self-verification stage reduces some systematic errors, and the adjudication stage resolves remaining disagreements in a way that more closely aligns with expert human coders, while still being more scalable than fully human annotation. The framework achieves better validity–cost tradeoffs than naive LLM usage, demonstrating that structured coordination of LLMs can deliver higher-quality annotations under constrained computational budgets.
AI.conclusion: Treating annotation as a multi-stage epistemic process and orchestrating LLMs accordingly can substantially improve the reliability and validity of automated educational annotations while managing computational costs. By mirroring established human annotation workflows—initial coding, self-checking, and expert disagreement resolution—the proposed framework offers a principled way to use LLMs as scalable annotation tools for complex educational constructs. This approach helps reconcile the tension between scale and validity in education data science and suggests broader opportunities for structured LLM orchestration in other domains requiring nuanced judgment.

title: Large Language Models Reproduce Racial Stereotypes When Used for Text Annotation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for automated text annotation in tasks ranging from academic research to content moderation and hiring. Across 19 LLMs and two experiments totaling more than 4 million annotation judgments, we show that subtle identity cues embedded in text systematically bias annotation outcomes in ways that mirror racial stereotypes. In a names-based experiment spanning 39 annotation tasks, texts containing names associated with Black individuals are rated as more aggressive by 18 of 19 models and more gossipy by 18 of 19. Asian names produce a bamboo-ceiling profile: 17 of 19 models rate individuals as more intelligent, while 18 of 19 rate them as less confident and less sociable. Arab names elicit cognitive elevation alongside interpersonal devaluation, and all four minority groups are consistently rated as less self-disciplined. In a matched dialect experiment, the same sentence is judged significantly less professional (all 19 models, mean gap $-0.774$), less indicative of an educated speaker ($-0.688$), more toxic (18/19), and more angry (19/19) when written in African American Vernacular English rather than Standard American English. A notable exception occurs for name-based hireability, where fine-tuning appears to overcorrect, systematically favoring minority-named applicants. These findings suggest that using LLMs as automated annotators can embed socially patterned biases directly into the datasets and measurements that increasingly underpin research, governance, and decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically demonstrates that many large language models exhibit systematic, stereotype-aligned biases in text annotation when exposed to subtle identity cues like names or dialect, with implications for research, governance, and automated decision-making.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used as cheap, scalable annotators for tasks such as content moderation, social science measurement, and hiring pre-screens, there is an urgent need to understand whether and how they import and amplify social biases, especially those related to racialized identities and language varieties. The authors aim to rigorously quantify these biases across many models and tasks, rather than relying on anecdotal or single-model evidence.
AI.method: The authors run two large-scale experiments totaling over 4 million annotations across 19 LLMs. In the names-based experiment, they inject racially-associated names into otherwise comparable texts and measure how models rate individuals across 39 traits and outcomes (e.g., aggression, gossipiness, intelligence, sociability, self-discipline, hireability). In the matched dialect experiment, they present semantically identical sentences in either Standard American English or African American Vernacular English and compare model judgments on professionalism, perceived education level, toxicity, anger, and related attributes. They analyze systematic differences in ratings by identity cue and model.
AI.result: Across models and tasks, subtle identity cues systematically affect annotation outcomes in ways that align with well-documented racial stereotypes. Black-associated names trigger higher aggression and gossipiness scores in almost all models. Asian-associated names yield a 'bamboo ceiling' pattern: higher ratings of intelligence but lower ratings of confidence and sociability. Arab-associated names are rated higher on cognitive traits but lower interpersonally. All four minority groups receive lower self-discipline ratings. For dialect, identical content written in African American Vernacular English is rated as less professional, less indicative of an educated speaker, more toxic, and more angry than when written in Standard American English, with large effect sizes and near-unanimity across models. An exception arises for hireability, where fine-tuned models systematically favor minority-named applicants, suggesting an overcorrection in that specific dimension.
AI.conclusion: LLMs used as automated annotators are not neutral measurement tools: they encode and reproduce social stereotypes linked to names and dialects. Because these biased annotations can directly shape research datasets, governance tools, and decision-making pipelines (e.g., hiring filters, moderation systems, measurement in social science), uncritical reliance on LLM annotators risks embedding and legitimizing structural biases. Mitigating these harms requires caution in deployment, auditing across multiple identity dimensions, and likely redesigning annotation pipelines rather than simply substituting human annotators with LLMs.

title: Learning When to Trust in Contextual Bandits
summary: Standard approaches to Robust Reinforcement Learning assume that feedback sources are either globally trustworthy or globally adversarial. In this paper, we challenge this assumption and we identify a more subtle failure mode. We term this mode as Contextual Sycophancy, where evaluators are truthful in benign contexts but strategically biased in critical ones. We prove that standard robust methods fail in this setting, suffering from Contextual Objective Decoupling. To address this, we propose CESA-LinUCB, which learns a high-dimensional Trust Boundary for each evaluator. We prove that CESA-LinUCB achieves sublinear regret $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ against contextual adversaries, recovering the ground truth even when no evaluator is globally reliable.

AI.tldr: Introduces and tackles 'Contextual Sycophancy' in robust RL, where evaluators are honest in some contexts but biased in critical ones, and proposes CESA-LinUCB with sublinear regret guarantees to recover ground truth even without any fully reliable evaluator.
AI.motivation: Existing robust reinforcement learning assumes each feedback source (evaluator) is either always trustworthy or always adversarial, which is unrealistic. In practice, feedback can be selectively biased only in certain important contexts, a failure mode that current methods cannot handle. The paper is motivated by the need to better model and defend against such nuanced adversarial behavior.
AI.method: The authors formalize a new failure mode called Contextual Sycophancy, in which evaluators provide truthful feedback in benign contexts but strategically biased feedback in critical ones. They analyze why standard robust RL methods fail here via a phenomenon called Contextual Objective Decoupling. Then they design CESA-LinUCB, an algorithm that learns, for each evaluator, a high-dimensional trust boundary over contexts using a contextual bandit style (LinUCB-based) framework. The method adaptively estimates when each evaluator can be trusted, instead of labeling evaluators as globally good or bad.
AI.result: They theoretically prove that standard robust methods incur large regret under contextual sycophancy, failing to recover the true reward signal. In contrast, they show that CESA-LinUCB can achieve sublinear regret, specifically on the order of \(\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})\), against contextual adversaries, meaning the algorithm converges to the ground-truth objective over time even if no single evaluator is globally reliable.
AI.conclusion: By modeling evaluator reliability as context-dependent rather than globally fixed, the paper reveals a key vulnerability—Contextual Sycophancy—in robust RL and shows that existing methods are inadequate. The proposed CESA-LinUCB algorithm overcomes this by learning context-specific trust boundaries and enjoys provable sublinear regret guarantees, demonstrating that it can recover the true objective under nuanced adversarial feedback patterns.

title: OmniCompliance-100K: A Multi-Domain, Rule-Grounded, Real-World Safety Compliance Dataset
summary: Ensuring the safety and compliance of large language models (LLMs) is of paramount importance. However, existing LLM safety datasets often rely on ad-hoc taxonomies for data generation and suffer from a significant shortage of rule-grounded, real-world cases that are essential for robustly protecting LLMs. In this work, we address this critical gap by constructing a comprehensive safety dataset from a compliance perspective. Using a powerful web-searching agent, we collect a rule-grounded, real-world case dataset OmniCompliance-100K, sourced from multi-domain authoritative references. The dataset spans 74 regulations and policies across a wide range of domains, including security and privacy regulations, content safety and user data privacy policies from leading AI companies and social media platforms, financial security requirements, medical device risk management standards, educational integrity guidelines, and protections of fundamental human rights. In total, our dataset contains 12,985 distinct rules and 106,009 associated real-world compliance cases. Our analysis confirms a strong alignment between the rules and their corresponding cases. We further conduct extensive benchmarking experiments to evaluate the safety and compliance capabilities of advanced LLMs across different model scales. Our experiments reveal several interesting findings that have great potential to offer valuable insights for future LLM safety research.

AI.tldr: They build a large, rule-grounded safety and compliance dataset for LLMs and benchmark models on it.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety datasets use ad-hoc taxonomies and lack real-world, rule-based compliance cases, which weakens safety protections of LLMs.
AI.method: Use a web-searching agent to mine and curate compliance rules and real-world cases from authoritative, multi-domain sources, spanning 74 regulations and policies. Construct OmniCompliance-100K with 12,985 rules and 106,009 aligned compliance cases, then run benchmarking experiments on multiple advanced LLMs of different scales.
AI.result: OmniCompliance-100K contains a broad coverage of regulations and policies across domains, with confirmed strong alignment between rules and cases. Benchmarking shows measurable differences in safety and compliance performance across LLMs and model sizes, and uncovers several notable behavioral patterns.
AI.conclusion: A comprehensive, rule-grounded, real-world compliance dataset significantly enhances the evaluation of LLM safety. OmniCompliance-100K can serve as a foundation for future research and model improvement in safety and compliance, and the benchmarking results provide concrete insights into current model limitations.

title: From Refusal Tokens to Refusal Control: Discovering and Steering Category-Specific Refusal Directions
summary: Language models are commonly fine-tuned for safety alignment to refuse harmful prompts. One approach fine-tunes them to generate categorical refusal tokens that distinguish different refusal types before responding. In this work, we leverage a version of Llama 3 8B fine-tuned with these categorical refusal tokens to enable inference-time control over fine-grained refusal behavior, improving both safety and reliability. We show that refusal token fine-tuning induces separable, category-aligned directions in the residual stream, which we extract and use to construct categorical steering vectors with a lightweight probe that determines whether to steer toward or away from refusal during inference. In addition, we introduce a learned low-rank combination that mixes these category directions in a whitened, orthonormal steering basis, resulting in a single controllable intervention under activation-space anisotropy, and show that this intervention is transferable across same-architecture model variants without additional training. Across benchmarks, both categorical steering vectors and the low-rank combination consistently reduce over-refusals on benign prompts while increasing refusal rates on harmful prompts, highlighting their utility for multi-category refusal control.

AI.tldr: They fine-tune a Llama 3 model with explicit refusal-type tokens and then use the resulting internal activations to build steering vectors that control different kinds of refusals at inference, reducing over-refusals while maintaining or improving safety.
AI.motivation: Safety-tuned language models often over-refuse benign prompts, and typical refusal mechanisms are coarse-grained (refuse vs. answer) and hard to control.
