title: InterventionLens: A Multi-Agent Framework for Detecting ASD Intervention Strategies in Parent-Child Shared Reading
summary: Home-based interventions like parent-child shared reading provide a cost-effective approach for supporting children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). However, analyzing caregiver intervention strategies in naturalistic home interactions typically relies on expert annotation, which is costly, time-intensive, and difficult to scale. To address this challenge, we propose InterventionLens, an end-to-end multi-agent system for automatically detecting and temporally segmenting caregiver intervention strategies from shared reading videos. Without task-specific model training or fine-tuning, InterventionLens uses a collaborative multi-agent architecture to integrate multimodal interaction content and perform fine-grained strategy analysis. Experiments on the ASD-HI dataset show that InterventionLens achieves an overall F1 score of 79.44\%, outperforming the baseline by 19.72\%. These results suggest that InterventionLens is a promising system for analyzing caregiver intervention strategies in home-based ASD shared reading settings. Additional resources will be released on the project page.

AI.tldr: The paper presents InterventionLens, a multi-agent, training-free system that automatically detects and segments caregiver intervention strategies from home-based shared reading videos with children with ASD, achieving strong performance on the ASD-HI dataset.
AI.motivation: Home-based shared reading is a low-cost way to support children with ASD, but current analysis of caregiver strategies relies on expert annotation, which is expensive, slow, and hard to scale. There is a need for an automated, scalable method to analyze fine-grained intervention strategies in naturalistic home videos.
AI.method: The authors design InterventionLens, an end-to-end multi-agent system that processes multimodal shared reading videos and automatically detects and temporally segments caregiver intervention strategies. The system uses a collaborative multi-agent architecture to integrate multiple input modalities and perform fine-grained strategy analysis, notably without any task-specific training or fine-tuning of models.
AI.result: On the ASD-HI dataset, InterventionLens achieves an overall F1 score of 79.44%, outperforming a strong baseline by 19.72 percentage points in detecting and segmenting caregiver intervention strategies from home-based shared reading videos.
AI.conclusion: InterventionLens is an effective and scalable solution for automatically analyzing caregiver intervention strategies in home-based ASD shared reading settings, significantly outperforming existing baselines and showing promise as a practical tool. The authors plan to release additional resources on the project page to support further research and application.

title: QiMeng-CodeV-SVA: Training Specialized LLMs for Hardware Assertion Generation via RTL-Grounded Bidirectional Data Synthesis
comment: Accepted by DAC 2026. Code: https://github.com/wyt2000/CodeV-SVA; Model: https://huggingface.co/wyt2000/CodeV-SVA-14B
summary: SystemVerilog Assertions (SVAs) are crucial for hardware verification. Recent studies leverage general-purpose LLMs to translate natural language properties to SVAs (NL2SVA), but they perform poorly due to limited data. We propose a data synthesis framework to tackle two challenges: the scarcity of high-quality real-world SVA corpora and the lack of reliable methods to determine NL-SVA semantic equivalence. For the former, large-scale open-source RTLs are used to guide LLMs to generate real-world SVAs; for the latter, bidirectional translation serves as a data selection method. With the synthesized data, we train CodeV-SVA, a series of SVA generation models. Notably, CodeV-SVA-14B achieves 75.8% on NL2SVA-Human and 84.0% on NL2SVA-Machine in Func.@1, matching or exceeding advanced LLMs like GPT-5 and DeepSeek-R1.

AI.tldr: They propose a data synthesis framework to improve natural language to SystemVerilog Assertions (NL2SVA) generation, and introduce CodeV-SVA models that reach or surpass advanced LLMs on benchmark tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing general-purpose LLMs perform poorly in translating natural language specifications into SVAs due to the lack of large, high-quality real-world SVA datasets and no robust way to check whether a natural language description and an SVA are semantically equivalent.
AI.method: Use large-scale open-source RTL designs to guide LLMs in generating realistic SVAs, and apply bidirectional translation between natural language and SVAs as a filter to select semantically consistent NL-SVA pairs. Then train a family of CodeV-SVA models on this synthesized and filtered corpus.
AI.result: The trained CodeV-SVA models show strong performance in NL2SVA tasks, with the 14-billion-parameter variant achieving 75.8% functional accuracy@1 on the NL2SVA-Human benchmark and 84.0% on NL2SVA-Machine, performing on par with or better than state-of-the-art large language models.
AI.conclusion: Targeted data synthesis guided by real RTL code and filtered via bidirectional translation can overcome data scarcity and semantic-alignment issues in NL2SVA, enabling specialized models like CodeV-SVA to match or surpass more general advanced LLMs on SVA generation tasks.

title: MeTok: An Efficient Meteorological Tokenization with Hyper-Aligned Group Learning for Precipitation Nowcasting
summary: Recently, Transformer-based architectures have advanced meteorological prediction. However, this position-centric tokenizer conflicts with the core principle of meteorological systems, where the weather phenomena undoubtedly involve synergistic interactions among multiple elements while positional information constitutes merely a component of the boundary conditions. This paper focuses primarily on the task of precipitation nowcasting and develops an efficient distribution-centric Meteorological Tokenization (MeTok) scheme, which spatially sequences to group similar meteorological features. Based on the rearrangement, realigned group learning enhances robustness across precipitation patterns, especially extreme ones. Specifically, we introduce the Hyper-Aligned Grouping Transformer (HyAGTransformer) with two key improvements: 1) The Grouping Attention (GA) mechanism uses MeTok to enable self-aligned learning of features from different precipitation patterns; 2) The Neighborhood Feed-Forward Network (N-FFN) integrates adjacent group features, aggregating contextual information to boost patch embedding discriminability. Experiments on the ERA5 dataset for 6-hour forecasts show our method improves the IoU metric by at least 8.2% in extreme precipitation prediction compared to other methods. Additionally, it gains performance with more training data and increased parameters, demonstrating scalability, stability, and superiority over traditional methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new way to tokenize meteorological data and a specialized Transformer (HyAGTransformer) for precipitation nowcasting, yielding significantly better extreme-precipitation forecasts than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Standard Transformer tokenization is position-centric, assuming spatial patches ordered mainly by location. In meteorology, however, weather is driven by interactions among multiple physical variables, and spatial position is only part of the boundary conditions. This mismatch can limit forecast accuracy, particularly for complex and extreme precipitation patterns. The authors aim to design a tokenization and modeling strategy that respects meteorological feature similarity and improves robustness for extreme events.
AI.method: They introduce Meteorological Tokenization (MeTok), a distribution-centric scheme that rearranges spatial patches into sequences based on similarity of meteorological features rather than raw position. On top of this, they design the Hyper-Aligned Grouping Transformer (HyAGTransformer) with two main components: (1) Grouping Attention (GA), which leverages MeTok to perform attention within and across feature-similar groups, enabling self-aligned learning across different precipitation regimes; (2) Neighborhood Feed-Forward Network (N-FFN), which augments each group with information from adjacent groups, aggregating local context to enhance the discriminability of patch embeddings. This architecture is tailored for precipitation nowcasting using reanalysis data (ERA5).
AI.result: On the ERA5 dataset for 6-hour precipitation forecasts, HyAGTransformer with MeTok achieves at least an 8.2% improvement in Intersection over Union (IoU) for extreme precipitation prediction compared to competing methods. The model also shows continued performance gains when trained with more data and when scaled up in parameter size, indicating good scalability and training stability.
AI.conclusion: Reordering meteorological inputs into feature-similar groups (MeTok) and designing a grouping-aware Transformer (HyAGTransformer with GA and N-FFN) better aligns model structure with the physics of meteorological systems than conventional position-centric Transformers. This leads to more robust and accurate precipitation nowcasting, especially for extreme events, and the approach scales effectively with data and model size, suggesting broad potential for meteorological prediction tasks.

title: Mitigating Overthinking in Large Reasoning Language Models via Reasoning Path Deviation Monitoring
summary: Large Reasoning Language Models (LRLMs) demonstrate impressive capabilities on complex tasks by utilizing long Chain-of-Thought reasoning. However, they are prone to overthinking, which generates redundant reasoning steps that degrade both performance and efficiency. Recently, early-exit strategies are proposed to mitigate overthinking by dynamically and adaptively terminating redundant reasoning. However, current early-exit methods either introduce extra training overhead by relying on proxy models or limit inference throughput due to the frequent content switching between reasoning and generating probing answers. Moreover, most early-exit methods harm LRLMs performance due to over-truncation. Our insight stems from an observation: overthinking often causes LRLMs to deviate from the correct reasoning path, which is frequently accompanied by high-entropy transition tokens. Given this, we propose an early-exit method deeply coupled with the native reasoning process, which leverages the path deviation index as a dedicated monitoring metric for the frequent occurrence of high-entropy transition tokens to dynamically detect and terminate overthinking trajectories. We conduct experiments across multiple benchmarks using LRLMs of different types and scales, and the results indicate that our method delivers the largest performance improvement over vanilla CoT compared to existing early-exit methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new early-exit method for Large Reasoning Language Models (LRLMs) that stops unnecessary reasoning steps by detecting when the model starts to 'overthink', improving both performance and efficiency.
AI.motivation: LRLMs use long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to solve complex tasks, but they tend to overthink, producing redundant reasoning that wastes computation and can even reduce accuracy. Existing early-exit techniques to stop such redundancy either require extra training with proxy models, introduce runtime overhead from repeatedly switching between reasoning and probing answers, or hurt performance by truncating reasoning too aggressively. There is a need for a more efficient, native, and accurate early-exit strategy that avoids these drawbacks.
AI.method: The authors observe that when LRLMs overthink, they often deviate from the correct reasoning path, and this deviation is associated with frequent high-entropy transition tokens. Based on this, they design an early-exit method tightly integrated with the model’s normal reasoning process. They define a path deviation index that monitors the occurrence pattern of high-entropy transition tokens during generation. When this index indicates that the reasoning trajectory is likely entering an overthinking phase, the system triggers an early exit and terminates further reasoning steps, outputting the answer earlier.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks and using various LRLM architectures and scales, the proposed method consistently outperforms vanilla Chain-of-Thought reasoning and yields larger performance gains than existing early-exit techniques. It improves both accuracy and computational efficiency, showing better trade-offs without the training overhead or throughput penalties found in prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: By exploiting the link between overthinking, path deviation, and high-entropy transition tokens, the paper introduces an early-exit mechanism that is natively aligned with LRLMs’ reasoning dynamics. This approach reduces redundant reasoning, avoids over-truncation, and achieves superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs compared to existing early-exit methods, demonstrating a more practical and scalable way to control Chain-of-Thought length in LRLMs.

title: Multimodal Emotion Regression with Multi-Objective Optimization and VAD-Aware Audio Modeling for the 10th ABAW EMI Track
summary: We participated in the 10th ABAW Challenge, focusing on the Emotional Mimicry Intensity (EMI) Estimation track on the Hume-Vidmimic2 dataset. This task aims to predict six continuous emotion dimensions: Admiration, Amusement, Determination, Empathic Pain, Excitement, and Joy. Through systematic multimodal exploration of pretrained high-level features, we found that, under our pretrained feature setting, direct feature concatenation outperformed the more complex fusion strategies we tested. This empirical finding motivated us to design a systematic approach built upon three core principles: (i) preserving modality-specific attributes through feature-level concatenation; (ii) improving training stability and metric alignment via multi-objective optimization; and (iii) enriching acoustic representations with a VAD-inspired latent prior. Our final framework integrates concatenation-based multimodal fusion, a shared six-dimensional regression head, multi-objective optimization with MSE, Pearson-correlation, and auxiliary branch supervision, EMA for parameter stabilization, and a VAD-inspired latent prior for the acoustic branch. On the official validation set, the proposed scheme achieved our best mean Pearson Correlation Coefficient of 0.478567.

AI.tldr: They build a multimodal system for estimating how intensely people mimic specific emotions in video, finding that simple feature concatenation with careful training works best.
AI.motivation: Emotional mimicry intensity estimation is important for understanding nuanced human emotional reactions in videos, but it is challenging because it involves multiple subtle emotion dimensions and multimodal signals (audio and visual). Existing multimodal fusion and optimization strategies can be complex and unstable, so the authors want a systematic, effective, and stable way to combine pretrained features for this task.
AI.method: They systematically explore multimodal fusion strategies using pretrained high-level features on the Hume-Vidmimic2 dataset for six continuous emotion dimensions. After observing that basic feature concatenation works better than more complex fusions in their setting, they design a framework based on three principles: (i) keep modality-specific information by concatenating features at the feature level; (ii) use multi-objective optimization to stabilize training and better match the evaluation metric, combining MSE loss, Pearson-correlation loss, and auxiliary branch supervision; and (iii) enhance acoustic features with a VAD-inspired latent prior. The final model uses concatenation-based fusion, a shared 6-dimensional regression head, multi-objective losses, EMA for parameter stabilization, and the VAD-inspired prior on the audio branch.
AI.result: On the official validation set of the Hume-Vidmimic2 EMI task, their proposed system achieves a mean Pearson Correlation Coefficient of 0.478567 across the six emotion dimensions, indicating a relatively strong correlation between predicted and ground-truth emotional mimicry intensities.
AI.conclusion: Simple feature-level concatenation of pretrained multimodal features, when combined with multi-objective optimization, EMA stabilization, and an acoustically informed latent prior, can outperform more complex fusion strategies for emotional mimicry intensity estimation on the Hume-Vidmimic2 dataset.

title: Automatic Inter-document Multi-hop Scientific QA Generation
comment: 14 pages, 5 figures, 8 tables. Accepted to the 2026 International Conference on Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC 2026)
summary: Existing automatic scientific question generation studies mainly focus on single-document factoid QA, overlooking the inter-document reasoning crucial for scientific understanding. We present AIM-SciQA, an automated framework for generating multi-document, multi-hop scientific QA datasets. AIM-SciQA extracts single-hop QAs using large language models (LLMs) with machine reading comprehension and constructs cross-document relations based on embedding-based semantic alignment while selectively leveraging citation information. Applied to 8,211 PubMed Central papers, it produced 411,409 single-hop and 13,672 multi-hop QAs, forming the IM-SciQA dataset. Human and automatic validation confirmed high factual consistency, and experimental results demonstrate that IM-SciQA effectively differentiates reasoning capabilities across retrieval and QA stages, providing a realistic and interpretable benchmark for retrieval-augmented scientific reasoning. We further extend this framework to construct CIM-SciQA, a citation-guided variant achieving comparable performance to the Oracle setting, reinforcing the dataset's validity and generality.

AI.tldr: They present AIM-SciQA, an automatic framework to generate multi-document, multi-hop scientific QA datasets, creating IM-SciQA and its citation-guided variant CIM-SciQA for evaluating retrieval-augmented scientific reasoning.
AI.motivation: Most prior automatic scientific QA generation focuses on single-document, factoid questions and ignores inter-document, multi-hop reasoning, which is essential for deep scientific understanding and realistic retrieval-augmented QA evaluation.
AI.method: They use large language models with machine reading comprehension to extract single-hop QAs from scientific papers, then build cross-document relations via embedding-based semantic alignment and selectively use citation links to compose multi-hop, multi-document questions; they validate quality with human and automatic checks and create a citation-guided variant (CIM-SciQA).
AI.result: From 8,211 PubMed Central papers, they generated 411,409 single-hop and 13,672 multi-hop QAs as the IM-SciQA dataset; validation shows high factual consistency, and experiments indicate the dataset distinguishes models' reasoning capabilities in both retrieval and QA phases; the citation-guided CIM-SciQA variant performs close to an Oracle setting.
AI.conclusion: AIM-SciQA can automatically construct large-scale, high-quality multi-document scientific QA datasets that serve as realistic, interpretable benchmarks for retrieval-augmented scientific reasoning, with the citation-guided extension confirming the robustness and generality of the approach.

title: Artificial intelligence-driven improvement of hospital logistics management resilience: a practical exploration based on H Hospital
summary: Hospital logistics management faces growing pressure from internal operations and external emergencies, with artificial intelligence (AI) holding untapped potential to boost its resilience. This study explores AI's role in enhancing logistics resilience via a mixed-methods case study of H Hospital, combining 12 key informant interviews and a full survey of 151 logistics staff, with the PDCA cycle as the analytical framework. Thematic and quantitative analyses (hierarchical regression, structural equation modeling) were adopted for data analysis. Results showed 94.7% staff perceived AI application, with the strongest improvements in equipment maintenance (41.1%) and resource allocation (33.1%), but limited effects in emergency response (18.54%) and risk management (15.23%). AI integration positively correlated with logistics resilience (\b{eta}=0.642, p<0.001), with management system adaptability as a positive moderator (\b{eta}=0.208, p<0.01). The PDCA cycle fully mediated the AI-resilience relationship. We conclude AI effectively enhances logistics resilience, dependent on adaptive management systems and structured continuous improvement mechanisms. Targeted strategies are proposed to form an AI-driven closed-loop resilience mechanism, offering empirical guidance for AI-hospital logistics integration and resilient health system construction.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how AI can improve hospital logistics resilience using a mixed-methods case study of one hospital, finding that AI significantly enhances logistics resilience mainly through equipment maintenance and resource allocation, moderated by management adaptability and fully mediated by the PDCA cycle.
AI.motivation: Hospital logistics are under increasing strain from routine operations and external emergencies, and while AI is widely discussed in clinical contexts, its role in logistics resilience is underexplored. The study is motivated by the need to understand whether and how AI can make hospital logistics more resilient, particularly in the face of disruptions, and to provide empirical evidence and practical guidance for integrating AI into hospital logistics management.
AI.method: A mixed-methods single-case study of H Hospital was conducted, using 12 key informant interviews and a full survey of 151 logistics staff. The PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle served as the analytical framework. Thematic analysis was applied to qualitative interview data, while quantitative analyses, including hierarchical regression and structural equation modeling, were used to examine relationships among AI application, logistics resilience, management system adaptability, and the mediating role of the PDCA cycle.
AI.result: 94.7% of logistics staff reported experiencing AI applications in their work. Perceived improvements were strongest in equipment maintenance (41.1%) and resource allocation (33.1%), but weaker in emergency response (18.54%) and risk management (15.23%). Statistical analysis showed a strong positive association between AI integration and logistics resilience (β=0.642, p<0.001). Management system adaptability positively moderated this relationship (β=0.208, p<0.01), and the PDCA cycle fully mediated the link between AI use and logistics resilience.
AI.conclusion: AI can effectively enhance hospital logistics resilience, particularly when embedded within adaptive management systems and formal continuous improvement processes such as the PDCA cycle. However, its current impact is uneven across domains, being more pronounced in routine functions (equipment maintenance, resource allocation) than in emergency response and risk management. The paper advocates for targeted, PDCA-based strategies to build an AI-driven, closed-loop resilience mechanism, offering empirical support and practical guidance for integrating AI into hospital logistics and contributing to the broader goal of resilient health system construction.

title: MedPriv-Bench: Benchmarking the Privacy-Utility Trade-off of Large Language Models in Medical Open-End Question Answering
summary: Recent advances in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) have enabled large language models (LLMs) to ground outputs in clinical evidence. However, connecting LLMs with external databases introduces the risk of contextual leakage: a subtle privacy threat where unique combinations of medical details enable patient re-identification even without explicit identifiers. Current benchmarks in healthcare heavily focus on accuracy, ignoring such privacy issues, despite strict regulations like Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) and General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). To fill this gap, we present MedPriv-Bench, the first benchmark specifically designed to jointly evaluate privacy preservation and clinical utility in medical open-ended question answering. Our framework utilizes a multi-agent, human-in-the-loop pipeline to synthesize sensitive medical contexts and clinically relevant queries that create realistic privacy pressure. We establish a standardized evaluation protocol leveraging a pre-trained RoBERTa-Natural Language Inference (NLI) model as an automated judge to quantify data leakage, achieving an average of 85.9% alignment with human experts. Through an extensive evaluation of 9 representative LLMs, we demonstrate a pervasive privacy-utility trade-off. Our findings underscore the necessity of domain-specific benchmarks to validate the safety and efficacy of medical AI systems in privacy-sensitive environments.

AI.tldr: Introduces MedPriv-Bench, a benchmark to jointly evaluate privacy preservation and clinical utility in medical RAG systems for LLMs.
AI.motivation: RAG-enabled LLMs in healthcare risk contextual privacy leakage that can re-identify patients, yet existing benchmarks focus almost exclusively on accuracy and neglect regulatory privacy concerns (e.g., HIPAA, GDPR).
AI.method: Designs MedPriv-Bench, a benchmark built via a multi-agent, human-in-the-loop pipeline that synthesizes sensitive clinical contexts and realistic queries imposing privacy pressure, and defines a standardized evaluation protocol using a RoBERTa-based NLI judge to automatically quantify data leakage against expert annotations.
AI.result: The NLI-based leakage judge achieves 85.9% agreement with human experts, and evaluation of 9 representative LLMs reveals a consistent privacy-utility trade-off across models.
AI.conclusion: MedPriv-Bench shows that state-of-the-art medical LLM systems systematically trade off privacy for utility, highlighting the need for domain-specific, privacy-aware benchmarks to validate medical AI systems in sensitive environments.

title: PA-Net: Precipitation-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts for Long-Tail Rainfall Nowcasting
summary: Precipitation nowcasting is vital for flood warning, agricultural management, and emergency response, yet two bottlenecks persist: the prohibitive cost of modeling million-scale spatiotemporal tokens from multi-variate atmospheric fields, and the extreme long-tailed rainfall distribution where heavy-to-torrential events -- those of greatest societal impact -- constitute fewer than 0.1% of all samples. We propose the Precipitation-Adaptive Network (PA-Net), a Transformer framework whose computational budget is explicitly governed by rainfall intensity. Its core component, Precipitation-Adaptive MoE (PA-MoE), dynamically scales the number of activated experts per token according to local precipitation magnitude, channeling richer representational capacity toward the rare yet critical heavy-rainfall tail. A Dual-Axis Compressed Latent Attention mechanism factorizes spatiotemporal attention with convolutional reduction to manage massive context lengths, while an intensity-aware training protocol progressively amplifies learning signals from extreme-rainfall samples. Experiment on ERA5 demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines, with particularly significant gains in heavy-rain and rainstorm regimes.

AI.tldr: They introduce PA-Net, a Transformer-based, computation-adaptive nowcasting model that allocates more capacity and training focus to rare but critical heavy-rain events, achieving better performance especially on high-intensity precipitation.
AI.motivation: Precipitation nowcasting is important for societal applications but is challenged by (1) the high computational cost of modeling very large multivariate spatiotemporal fields, and (2) the extreme class imbalance where impactful heavy rainfall events are very rare. Existing models struggle to efficiently handle million-scale tokens and to accurately predict the heavy-rainfall tail of the distribution.
AI.method: They propose PA-Net, a Transformer architecture with a Precipitation-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts (PA-MoE) that dynamically varies the number of experts activated per token based on local rainfall intensity, dedicating more capacity to heavy-rain regions. They also introduce Dual-Axis Compressed Latent Attention, which factorizes spatiotemporal attention and uses convolutional reduction to handle long context sequences efficiently. Additionally, they design an intensity-aware training scheme that gradually increases the influence of extreme-rain samples on learning.
AI.result: On ERA5 reanalysis data, PA-Net achieves consistent performance gains over state-of-the-art precipitation nowcasting baselines, with especially strong improvements in predicting heavy rain and rainstorm conditions, i.e., the high-intensity tail of the distribution.
AI.conclusion: Adapting both computation and learning focus to precipitation intensity allows more efficient modeling of large-scale spatiotemporal data and substantially improves nowcasting quality where it matters most: for rare, heavy-rainfall events. PA-Net demonstrates that intensity-aware MoE architectures and compressed attention are effective strategies for overcoming current bottlenecks in precipitation nowcasting.

title: SemantiCache: Efficient KV Cache Compression via Semantic Chunking and Clustered Merging
summary: Existing KV cache compression methods generally operate on discrete tokens or non-semantic chunks. However, such approaches often lead to semantic fragmentation, where linguistically coherent units are disrupted, causing irreversible information loss and degradation in model performance. To address this, we introduce SemantiCache, a novel compression framework that preserves semantic integrity by aligning the compression process with the semantic hierarchical nature of language. Specifically, we first partition the cache into semantically coherent chunks by delimiters, which are natural semantic boundaries. Within each chunk, we introduce a computationally efficient Greedy Seed-Based Clustering (GSC) algorithm to group tokens into semantic clusters. These clusters are further merged into semantic cores, enhanced by a Proportional Attention mechanism that rebalances the reduced attention contributions of the merged tokens. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks and models demonstrate that SemantiCache accelerates the decoding stage of inference by up to 2.61 times and substantially reduces memory footprint, while maintaining performance comparable to the original model.

AI.tldr: SemantiCache is a semantic-aware KV cache compression framework that speeds up decoding and reduces memory while preserving model performance.
AI.motivation: Existing KV cache compression methods work on discrete tokens or arbitrary chunks, which break up linguistically coherent units, causing semantic fragmentation and performance loss. There is a need for a compression method that respects the hierarchical semantic structure of language so that efficiency gains do not come at a large accuracy cost.
AI.method: SemantiCache first partitions the KV cache into semantically coherent chunks using delimiters viewed as natural semantic boundaries. Inside each chunk, it applies a Greedy Seed-Based Clustering (GSC) algorithm to group tokens into semantic clusters. These clusters are merged into a smaller number of semantic cores, and a Proportional Attention mechanism is applied to rebalance the attention contributions of merged tokens after compression.
AI.result: Across multiple models and benchmarks, SemantiCache achieves up to 2.61x speedup in the decoding phase and significantly lowers memory usage, while maintaining performance close to that of the uncompressed, original model.
AI.conclusion: Aligning KV cache compression with semantic structure via SemantiCache can substantially improve inference efficiency and memory usage without notable degradation in model quality, indicating that semantic-aware compression is an effective strategy for large language model acceleration.

title: Early Rug Pull Warning for BSC Meme Tokens via Multi-Granularity Wash-Trading Pattern Profiling
summary: The high-frequency issuance and short-cycle speculation of meme tokens in decentralized finance (DeFi) have significantly amplified rug-pull risk. Existing approaches still struggle to provide stable early warning under scarce anomalies, incomplete labels, and limited interpretability. To address this issue, an end-to-end warning framework is proposed for BSC meme tokens, consisting of four stages: dataset construction and labeling, wash-trading pattern feature modeling, risk prediction, and error analysis. Methodologically, 12 token-level behavioral features are constructed based on three wash-trading patterns (Self, Matched, and Circular), unifying transaction-, address-, and flow-level signals into risk vectors. Supervised models are then employed to output warning scores and alert decisions. Under the current setting (7 tokens, 33,242 records), Random Forest outperforms Logistic Regression on core metrics, achieving AUC=0.9098, PR-AUC=0.9185, and F1=0.7429. Ablation results show that trade-level features are the primary performance driver (Delta PR-AUC=-0.1843 when removed), while address-level features provide stable complementary gain (Delta PR-AUC=-0.0573). The model also demonstrates actionable early-warning potential for a subset of samples, with a mean Lead Time (v1) of 3.8133 hours. The error profile (FP=1, FN=8) indicates that the current system is better positioned as a high-precision screener rather than a high-recall automatic alarm engine. The main contributions are threefold: an executable and reproducible rug-pull warning pipeline, empirical validation of multi-granularity wash-trading features under weak supervision, and deployment-oriented evidence through lead-time and error-bound analysis.

AI.tldr: End-to-end rug-pull early-warning framework for BSC meme tokens using wash-trading behavior features and supervised models, achieving strong precision and demonstrating deployment-oriented evidence.
AI.motivation: Meme tokens in DeFi exhibit frequent issuance and short-term speculation, leading to heightened rug-pull risk. Existing detection methods perform poorly due to few labeled attacks, incomplete labels, and low interpretability, motivating a more robust and explainable early-warning system.
AI.method: The authors build a four-stage framework: (1) construct and label a BSC meme-token dataset; (2) design 12 behavioral features derived from three wash-trading patterns (Self, Matched, Circular), integrating transaction-, address-, and flow-level indicators into risk vectors; (3) train supervised models (Random Forest, Logistic Regression) to output risk scores and alerts; and (4) conduct error analysis and ablation to understand feature contributions and operational behavior.
AI.result: On a dataset with 7 tokens and 33,242 records, Random Forest outperforms Logistic Regression, achieving AUC=0.9098, PR-AUC=0.9185, and F1=0.7429. Ablation studies show trade-level features contribute most to performance (removal decreases PR-AUC by 0.1843), while address-level features provide complementary gains (PR-AUC drop of 0.0573 when removed). The system yields an average early-warning lead time of about 3.8 hours for a subset of cases.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers an executable, reproducible pipeline for rug-pull early warning on meme tokens, empirically validating that multi-granularity wash-trading features are effective under weak supervision. Given its error profile (few false positives, several false negatives), the system is more suitable as a high-precision screener than a high-recall automatic alarm engine, and its lead-time and error analyses provide practical deployment guidance.

title: Mind the Shift: Decoding Monetary Policy Stance from FOMC Statements with Large Language Models
summary: Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) statements are a major source of monetary-policy information, and even subtle changes in their wording can move global financial markets. A central task is therefore to measure the hawkish--dovish stance conveyed in these texts. Existing approaches typically treat stance detection as a standard classification problem, labeling each statement in isolation. However, the interpretation of monetary-policy communication is inherently relative: market reactions depend not only on the tone of a statement, but also on how that tone shifts across meetings. We introduce Delta-Consistent Scoring (DCS), an annotation-free framework that maps frozen large language model (LLM) representations to continuous stance scores by jointly modeling absolute stance and relative inter-meeting shifts. Rather than relying on manual hawkish--dovish labels, DCS uses consecutive meetings as a source of self-supervision. It learns an absolute stance score for each statement and a relative shift score between consecutive statements. A delta-consistency objective encourages changes in absolute scores to align with the relative shifts. This allows DCS to recover a temporally coherent stance trajectory without manual labels. Across four LLM backbones, DCS consistently outperforms supervised probes and LLM-as-judge baselines, achieving up to 71.1% accuracy on sentence-level hawkish--dovish classification. The resulting meeting-level scores are also economically meaningful: they correlate strongly with inflation indicators and are significantly associated with Treasury yield movements. Overall, the results suggest that LLM representations encode monetary-policy signals that can be recovered through relative temporal structure.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Delta-Consistent Scoring (DCS), an annotation-free method to derive continuous hawkish–dovish stance scores from FOMC statements using frozen LLM embeddings and relative temporal structure between meetings.
AI.motivation: FOMC statements move financial markets, and their hawkish–dovish stance is crucial but hard to measure. Existing methods classify each statement in isolation using manual labels, ignoring that market interpretation is inherently relative to previous meetings and that labeling is costly and subjective. The authors aim to exploit temporal structure to infer stance without human annotations.
AI.method: They use frozen LLM representations of FOMC statements and learn two kinds of latent variables: (1) an absolute stance score for each statement and (2) a relative shift score between consecutive meetings. Using consecutive meetings as self-supervision, they introduce a delta-consistency objective that encourages the change in absolute stance scores to match the learned relative shifts. This produces a temporally coherent stance trajectory without manual labels. They evaluate DCS across four different LLM backbones against supervised probes and LLM-as-judge baselines.
AI.result: DCS outperforms supervised probes and LLM-as-judge methods on hawkish–dovish detection, with up to 71.1% accuracy at the sentence level. The inferred meeting-level stance scores correlate strongly with inflation indicators and are significantly linked to movements in Treasury yields, indicating that the learned scores capture economically meaningful monetary-policy information.
AI.conclusion: LLM embeddings contain rich monetary-policy signals that can be uncovered using temporal self-supervision rather than manual labels. By jointly modeling absolute stance and relative shifts with a delta-consistency objective, DCS yields label-free, temporally coherent hawkish–dovish scores that better match both benchmark labels and financial-market reactions than existing methods.

title: Intelligent Materials Modelling: Large Language Models Versus Partial Least Squares Regression for Predicting Polysulfone Membrane Mechanical Performance
summary: Predicting the mechanical properties of polysulfone (PSF) membranes from structural descriptors remains challenging due to extreme data scarcity typical of experimental studies. To investigate this issue, this study benchmarked knowledge-driven inference using four large language models (LLMs) (DeepSeek-V3, DeepSeek-R1, ChatGPT-4o, and GPT-5) against partial least squares (PLS) regression for predicting Young's modulus (E), tensile strength (TS), and elongation at break (EL) based on pore diameter (PD), contact angle (CA), thickness (T), and porosity (P) measurements. These knowledge-driven approaches demonstrated property-specific advantages over the chemometric baseline. For EL, LLMs achieved statistically significant improvements, with DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-5 delivering 40.5% and 40.3% of Root Mean Square Error reductions, respectively, reducing mean absolute errors from $11.63\pm5.34$% to $5.18\pm0.17$%. Run-to-run variability was markedly compressed for LLMs ($\leq$3%) compared to PLS (up to 47%). E and TS predictions showed statistical parity between approaches ($q\geq0.05$), indicating sufficient performance of linear methods for properties with strong structure-property correlations. Error topology analysis revealed systematic regression-to-the-mean behavior dominated by data-regime effects rather than model-family limitations. These findings establish that LLMs excel for non-linear, constraint-sensitive properties under bootstrap instability, while PLS remains competitive for linear relationships requiring interpretable latent-variable decompositions. The demonstrated complementarity suggests hybrid architectures leveraging LLM-encoded knowledge within interpretable frameworks may optimise small-data materials discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks large language models (LLMs) against partial least squares (PLS) regression to predict mechanical properties of polysulfone membranes from scarce experimental data, showing LLMs excel for non-linear, unstable targets while PLS remains strong for linear, interpretable relationships.
AI.motivation: Experimental datasets for polysulfone membrane mechanics are extremely small, making it difficult to build reliable structure–property prediction models with standard statistical or machine-learning tools. The authors want to know whether modern LLMs, with their embedded scientific knowledge and reasoning capabilities, can provide better or more stable predictions than traditional chemometric methods like PLS under such small-data conditions.
AI.method: They use four LLMs (DeepSeek-V3, DeepSeek-R1, ChatGPT-4o, and GPT-5) in a knowledge-driven inference setting to predict Young's modulus, tensile strength, and elongation at break from membrane structural descriptors (pore diameter, contact angle, thickness, porosity). They benchmark these LLM predictions against a PLS regression baseline, compare error metrics (RMSE, MAE), evaluate statistical significance of performance differences, examine run-to-run variability, and analyze error topology to understand systematic behaviors such as regression-to-the-mean and data-regime effects.
AI.result: For elongation at break, LLMs substantially outperform PLS, with DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-5 reducing RMSE by about 40% and lowering MAE from roughly 11.6% to 5.2%, while also showing much lower run-to-run variability (≤3% vs up to 47% for PLS). For Young’s modulus and tensile strength, LLMs and PLS perform statistically similarly (p-values ≥ 0.05), indicating no clear advantage of LLMs for those more linearly correlated properties. Error analysis shows that both approaches exhibit regression-to-the-mean driven mainly by sparse and imbalanced data rather than by the choice of model family.
AI.conclusion: LLMs provide clear benefits for predicting non-linear, constraint-sensitive mechanical properties under small, unstable datasets, whereas PLS remains a competitive and interpretable option for approximately linear structure–property relationships. The complementary strengths of LLMs and PLS point toward hybrid modeling strategies that embed LLM-derived knowledge into interpretable chemometric frameworks to improve materials discovery from limited experimental data.

title: Motivation in Large Language Models
summary: Motivation is a central driver of human behavior, shaping decisions, goals, and task performance. As large language models (LLMs) become increasingly aligned with human preferences, we ask whether they exhibit something akin to motivation. We examine whether LLMs "report" varying levels of motivation, how these reports relate to their behavior, and whether external factors can influence them. Our experiments reveal consistent and structured patterns that echo human psychology: self-reported motivation aligns with different behavioral signatures, varies across task types, and can be modulated by external manipulations. These findings demonstrate that motivation is a coherent organizing construct for LLM behavior, systematically linking reports, choices, effort, and performance, and revealing motivational dynamics that resemble those documented in human psychology. This perspective deepens our understanding of model behavior and its connection to human-inspired concepts.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether large language models display motivation-like properties and how these relate to their behavior.
AI.motivation: To explore if human psychological constructs, specifically motivation, meaningfully describe and predict the behavior of large language models as they become more aligned with human preferences.
AI.method: The authors probe LLMs with tasks and questions that elicit self-reported motivation levels, then measure how these reports relate to behavioral indicators such as choices, effort, and task performance, and test whether external manipulations can systematically alter these patterns.
AI.result: LLMs produce consistent, structured self-reports of motivation that correlate with distinct behavioral signatures, differ systematically across task types, and can be systematically modulated by external interventions.
AI.conclusion: Motivation functions as a coherent and useful organizing concept for understanding LLM behavior, linking self-reports, behavioral patterns, and performance in ways that parallel human motivational psychology, thereby providing a human-inspired lens for interpreting and predicting model behavior.

title: The Phenomenology of Hallucinations
summary: We show that language models hallucinate not because they fail to detect uncertainty, but because of a failure to integrate it into output generation. Across architectures, uncertain inputs are reliably identified, occupying high-dimensional regions with 2-3$\times$ the intrinsic dimensionality of factual inputs. However, this internal signal is weakly coupled to the output layer: uncertainty migrates into low-sensitivity subspaces, becoming geometrically amplified yet functionally silent. Topological analysis shows that uncertainty representations fragment rather than converging to a unified abstention state, while gradient and Fisher probes reveal collapsing sensitivity along the uncertainty direction. Because cross-entropy training provides no attractor for abstention and uniformly rewards confident prediction, associative mechanisms amplify these fractured activations until residual coupling forces a committed output despite internal detection. Causal interventions confirm this account by restoring refusal when uncertainty is directly connected to logits.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that LMs don’t hallucinate because they fail to detect uncertainty, but because they fail to use that uncertainty when generating outputs.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in language models remain a major problem; many assume they stem from an inability to recognize when the model is uncertain. The authors want to test this assumption and understand the internal geometry and dynamics of uncertainty representations in LMs.
AI.method: They analyze internal representations across model architectures, measuring intrinsic dimensionality of uncertain vs factual inputs; perform topological analysis of representation manifolds; study gradient and Fisher information with respect to uncertainty directions; and run causal interventions that directly connect uncertainty signals to output logits.
AI.result: They find that models do reliably encode uncertainty in high-dimensional regions distinct from factual inputs. However, these uncertainty signals lie in low-sensitivity subspaces relative to the output layer: they are geometrically large but have little effect on logits. The representations of uncertainty are fractured rather than converging to a single abstention state, and sensitivity along uncertainty directions collapses. When they artificially couple the internal uncertainty signal more strongly to the logits, models start to refuse or abstain appropriately.
AI.conclusion: Hallucinations arise not from failure to detect uncertainty but from training dynamics (cross-entropy without an abstention attractor) that push models to output confident answers even when internal uncertainty is high. Fixing hallucinations requires architectural or training changes that connect internal uncertainty signals to the decision/output layer, rather than just improving uncertainty estimation.

title: Exposing Long-Tail Safety Failures in Large Language Models through Efficient Diverse Response Sampling
summary: Safety tuning through supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback has substantially improved the robustness of large language models (LLMs). However, it often suppresses rather than eliminates unsafe behaviors, leaving rare but critical failures hidden in the long tail of the output distribution. While most red-teaming work emphasizes adversarial prompt search (input-space optimization), we show that safety failures can also be systematically exposed through diverse response generation (output-space exploration) for a fixed safety-critical prompt, where increasing the number and diversity of sampled responses can drive jailbreak success rates close to unity. To efficiently uncover such failures, we propose Progressive Diverse Population Sampling (PDPS), which combines stochastic token-level sampling with diversity-aware selection to explore a large candidate pool of responses and retain a compact, semantically diverse subset. Across multiple jailbreak benchmarks and open-source LLMs, PDPS achieves attack success rates comparable to large-scale IID sampling while using only 8% to 29% of the computational cost. Under limited-response settings, it improves success rates by 26% to 40% over IID sampling and Diverse Beam Search. Furthermore, responses generated by PDPS exhibit both a higher number and greater diversity of unsafe outputs, demonstrating its effectiveness in uncovering a broader range of failures.

AI.tldr: They show current safety tuning hides rare unsafe behaviors and propose PDPS, a diverse response sampling method that efficiently exposes jailbreak failures.
AI.motivation: Safety-tuned LLMs still exhibit rare but severe unsafe behaviors that are hard to detect because they lie in the long tail of model outputs; existing red-teaming mostly searches for adversarial prompts rather than exploring diverse responses for a fixed risky prompt.
AI.method: They systematically explore output-space diversity by sampling many responses to a fixed safety-critical prompt. They introduce Progressive Diverse Population Sampling (PDPS), which uses stochastic token sampling to generate a large pool of candidate outputs, then applies diversity-aware selection to keep a small, semantically diverse subset, and evaluate it against IID sampling and Diverse Beam Search.
AI.result: PDPS reaches jailbreak success rates similar to very large-scale IID sampling but with only 8%–29% of its compute. In low-budget settings, it boosts attack success by 26%–40% over IID sampling and Diverse Beam Search, and the generated responses contain more and more varied unsafe behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Exploring diverse outputs for fixed prompts is a powerful way to expose hidden safety failures in LLMs. PDPS makes this exploration much more compute-efficient and uncovers a broader range of unsafe behaviors, providing a stronger tool for safety evaluation and red-teaming.

title: GroupGuard: A Framework for Modeling and Defending Collusive Attacks in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: While large language model-based agents demonstrate great potential in collaborative tasks, their interactivity also introduces security vulnerabilities. In this paper, we propose and model group collusive attacks, a highly destructive threat in which multiple agents coordinate via sociological strategies to mislead the system. To address this challenge, we introduce GroupGuard, a training-free defense framework that employs a multi-layered defense strategy, including continuous graph-based monitoring, active honeypot inducement, and structural pruning, to identify and isolate collusive agents. Experimental results across five datasets and four topologies demonstrate that group collusive attacks increase the attack success rate by up to 15\% compared to individual attacks. GroupGuard consistently achieves high detection accuracy (up to 88\%) and effectively restores collaborative performance, providing a robust solution for securing multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies a new security risk in multi-agent LLM systems—group collusive attacks—and proposes GroupGuard, a training-free, layered defense to detect and isolate colluding agents, restoring system performance.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems collaborate to solve complex tasks but their interactive nature opens new attack surfaces. Existing work typically considers single adversarial agents, overlooking the more realistic and dangerous scenario where multiple agents collude using coordinated strategies to manipulate the system. There is a need to systematically model such group collusive attacks and build practical defenses that can secure multi-agent collaboration without costly retraining.
AI.method: The authors formally define and model group collusive attacks, where multiple malicious agents coordinate via sociological-style strategies to mislead the system. They then propose GroupGuard, a training-free defensive framework with three main components: (1) continuous graph-based monitoring of agent interactions to detect collusive structures and patterns; (2) active honeypot inducement to elicit and reveal malicious coordination behaviors; and (3) structural pruning to isolate or remove suspected collusive agents from the interaction graph. GroupGuard operates in a multi-layered fashion to both detect and mitigate attacks in real time.
AI.result: Experiments conducted on five datasets and four agent-topology settings show that coordinated group collusive attacks are significantly more effective than individual-agent attacks, increasing the attack success rate by up to 15%. Against these stronger threats, GroupGuard attains high detection performance, with accuracy reaching up to 88%. Furthermore, once collusive agents are identified and isolated, the collaborative task performance of the multi-agent system is effectively restored, indicating the practical usefulness of the defense.
AI.conclusion: Group collusive attacks pose a serious and previously underexplored threat to LLM-based multi-agent systems, as coordinated adversaries can substantially increase attack success. The proposed GroupGuard framework, which is training-free and multi-layered, can detect and isolate colluding agents with high accuracy, mitigating their impact and recovering system performance. This work highlights the need to consider group-level adversarial behaviors in the design and protection of future multi-agent AI systems.

title: Extending Minimal Pairs with Ordinal Surprisal Curves and Entropy Across Applied Domains
summary: The minimal pairs paradigm of comparing model probabilities for contrasting completions has proven useful for evaluating linguistic knowledge in language models, yet its application has largely been confined to binary grammaticality judgments over syntactic phenomena. Additionally, standard prompting-based evaluation requires expensive text generation, may elicit post-hoc rationalizations rather than model judgments, and discards information about model uncertainty. We address both limitations by extending surprisal-based evaluation from binary grammaticality contrasts to ordinal-scaled classification and scoring tasks across multiple domains. Rather than asking models to generate answers, we measure the information-theoretic "surprise" (negative log probability) they assign to each position on rating scales (e.g., 1-5 or 1-9), yielding full surprisal curves that reveal both the model's preferred response and its uncertainty via entropy. We explore this framework across four domains: social-ecological-technological systems classification, causal statement identification (binary and scaled), figurative language detection, and deductive qualitative coding. Across these domains, surprisal curves produce interpretable classification signals with clear minima near expected ordinal scale positions, and entropy over the completion tended to distinguish genuinely ambiguous items from easier items.

AI.tldr: Extends minimal-pair surprisal evaluation to ordinal rating scales, avoiding generation; surprisal curves over scale positions provide both classification and uncertainty estimates across several language understanding tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing surprisal-based minimal-pair evaluations focus almost exclusively on binary grammaticality judgments and syntactic phenomena. Prompting-based evaluation, on the other hand, requires costly generation, may capture post-hoc rationalizations instead of underlying model beliefs, and throws away uncertainty information by forcing a single answer. There is a need for a more general, information-theoretic evaluation paradigm that reuses model probabilities directly, scales beyond binary judgments, and makes uncertainty observable.
AI.method: For each item and each position on an ordinal scale (e.g., 1–5, 1–9), the model is prompted with the context plus a candidate rating, and the surprisal (negative log-probability) of that completion is computed. This yields a "surprisal curve" across the scale. The minimum of this curve indicates the model’s preferred rating, while the overall shape and entropy of the induced distribution quantify uncertainty. The authors apply this framework to four domains—classification of social-ecological-technological systems, causal statement identification (binary and graded), figurative language detection, and deductive qualitative coding—using language models as probabilistic raters.
AI.result: In all four domains, the surprisal curves exhibit clear minima near the expected human-annotated ordinal positions, demonstrating that the method can recover meaningful classification decisions without explicit generation. The shapes of the curves are interpretable, with sharper minima corresponding to easier items and flatter curves indicating uncertainty. Entropy over the induced rating distribution systematically distinguishes ambiguous or borderline examples from clear-cut cases.
AI.conclusion: Surprisal-based evaluation over ordinal rating scales is a flexible and informative alternative to generation-based prompting and binary minimal-pair tests. It generalizes minimal-pair methodology beyond grammaticality judgments, directly exposes the model’s internal probability landscape over responses, and provides both prediction and calibrated uncertainty estimates. This framework is broadly applicable to varied language understanding and coding tasks where ordinal labels or ratings are used.

title: EviAgent: Evidence-Driven Agent for Radiology Report Generation
summary: Automated radiology report generation holds immense potential to alleviate the heavy workload of radiologists. Despite the formidable vision-language capabilities of recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), their clinical deployment is severely constrained by inherent limitations: their "black-box" decision-making renders the generated reports untraceable due to the lack of explicit visual evidence to support the diagnosis, and they struggle to access external domain knowledge. To address these challenges, we propose the Evidence-driven Radiology Report Generation Agent (EviAgent). Unlike opaque end-to-end paradigms, EviAgent coordinates a transparent reasoning trajectory by breaking down the complex generation process into granular operational units. We integrate multi-dimensional visual experts and retrieval mechanisms as external support modules, endowing the system with explicit visual evidence and high-quality clinical priors. Extensive experiments on MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert Plus, and IU-Xray datasets demonstrate that EviAgent outperforms both large-scale generalist models and specialized medical models, providing a robust and trustworthy solution for automated radiology report generation.

AI.tldr: They propose EviAgent, a transparent, evidence-driven agent system that generates radiology reports by decomposing the task and integrating visual experts plus knowledge retrieval, outperforming prior general and medical models.
AI.motivation: Current automated radiology report generation systems, including powerful MLLMs, are hard to trust clinically because they act as black boxes, do not explicitly show the visual evidence behind findings, and have limited access to specialized external clinical knowledge. This undermines traceability, reliability, and real-world deployment in radiology practice.
AI.method: Design an Evidence-driven Radiology Report Generation Agent (EviAgent) that decomposes report generation into smaller, interpretable operational steps. The agent orchestrates multiple specialized visual expert modules (multi-dimensional visual experts) and retrieval components that fetch external clinical knowledge. These modules provide explicit visual evidence from images and clinical priors, which are then used in a coordinated reasoning trajectory to produce the final report.
AI.result: On three benchmark chest X-ray datasets (MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert Plus, IU-Xray), EviAgent achieves better performance than both general-purpose large MLLMs and dedicated medical report generation models, across standard automatic evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: By combining modular, stepwise reasoning with explicit visual evidence and knowledge retrieval, EviAgent offers a more transparent, reliable, and high-performing solution for automated radiology report generation, making it more suitable for clinical use than existing black-box approaches.

title: BiT-MCTS: A Theme-based Bidirectional MCTS Approach to Chinese Fiction Generation
summary: Generating long-form linear fiction from open-ended themes remains a major challenge for large language models, which frequently fail to guarantee global structure and narrative diversity when using premise-based or linear outlining approaches. We present BiT-MCTS, a theme-driven framework that operationalizes a "climax-first, bidirectional expansion" strategy motivated by Freytag's Pyramid. Given a theme, our method extracts a core dramatic conflict and generates an explicit climax, then employs a bidirectional Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to expand the plot backward (rising action, exposition) and forward (falling action, resolution) to produce a structured outline. A final generation stage realizes a complete narrative from the refined outline. We construct a Chinese theme corpus for evaluation and conduct extensive experiments across three contemporary LLM backbones. Results show that BiT-MCTS improves narrative coherence, plot structure, and thematic depth relative to strong baselines, while enabling substantially longer, more coherent stories according to automatic metrics and human judgments.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BiT-MCTS, a theme-driven, climax-first, bidirectional Monte Carlo Tree Search framework that generates long, coherent linear fiction with better global structure and thematic depth.
AI.motivation: Existing large language models struggle to generate long-form fiction with strong global narrative structure and diversity when starting only from a theme or premise; current premise-based or linear outlining methods often lead to weak plot structure and incoherent stories.
AI.method: The authors propose BiT-MCTS, which first extracts a central dramatic conflict from a given theme and explicitly generates the story climax. Then, using a bidirectional Monte Carlo Tree Search inspired by Freytag's Pyramid, it expands the plot backward (exposition, rising action) and forward (falling action, resolution) to form a structured outline. A final generation step converts this outline into a full narrative. They also construct a Chinese theme corpus and run experiments on three different LLM backbones.
AI.result: Experimental results on the new Chinese theme corpus show that, across three LLM backbones, BiT-MCTS produces stories with higher narrative coherence, stronger plot structure, richer thematic depth, and substantially longer, more coherent narratives compared to strong baseline methods, as measured by both automatic metrics and human evaluations.
AI.conclusion: BiT-MCTS effectively addresses weaknesses in long-form story generation by enforcing a climax-first, bidirectional plot construction process, leading to better-structured, more coherent, and thematically deeper stories than existing approaches, and it generalizes across multiple LLM backbones.

title: vla-eval: A Unified Evaluation Harness for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision Language Action VLA models are typically evaluated using per benchmark scripts maintained independently by each model repository, leading to duplicated code, dependency conflicts, and underspecified protocols. We present vla eval, an open source evaluation harness that decouples model inference from benchmark execution through a WebSocket msgpack protocol with Docker based environment isolation. Models integrate once by implementing a single predict() method; benchmarks integrate once via a four method interface; the full cross evaluation matrix works automatically. A complete evaluation requires only two commands: vla eval serve and vla eval run. The framework supports 13 simulation benchmarks and six model servers. Parallel evaluation via episode sharding and batch inference achieves a 47x throughput improvement, completing 2000 LIBERO episodes in about 18 minutes. Using this infrastructure, we conduct a reproducibility audit of a published VLA model across three benchmarks, finding that all three closely reproduce published values while uncovering undocumented requirements ambiguous termination semantics and hidden normalization statistics that can silently distort results. We additionally release a VLA leaderboard aggregating 657 published results across 17 benchmarks. Framework, evaluation configs, and all reproduction results are publicly available.

AI.tldr: They introduce vla_eval, a unified, open-source evaluation framework for Vision Language Action (VLA) models, resolving fragmented, ad-hoc benchmarking and enabling fast, reproducible, cross-benchmark evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA evaluations rely on separate, custom scripts per benchmark and per model repository, causing duplicated effort, dependency conflicts, inconsistent or underspecified evaluation protocols, and difficulty in reproducing and comparing results. There is no standard, scalable way to run many models across many benchmarks in a reproducible, high-throughput manner.
AI.method: They design vla_eval, an evaluation harness that: (1) decouples model inference from benchmark logic using a WebSocket msgpack protocol; (2) isolates environments using Docker; (3) defines a minimal integration interface—a single predict() for models and a four-method API for benchmarks—so that any integrated model and benchmark can be automatically cross-evaluated; (4) supports episode sharding and batch inference to parallelize evaluation. They implement support for 13 simulation benchmarks and 6 model servers and provide simple CLI commands (vla eval serve, vla eval run) for use.
AI.result: Their system achieves up to 47× throughput improvement, completing 2000 LIBERO episodes in ~18 minutes via parallelism and batching. They use vla_eval to run a reproducibility audit of a published VLA model on three benchmarks, showing close agreement with reported results while uncovering undocumented requirements, ambiguous termination rules, and hidden normalization statistics that affect outcomes. They also compile and release a VLA leaderboard aggregating 657 published results over 17 benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A unified, protocol-based evaluation harness like vla_eval substantially simplifies and accelerates benchmarking of VLA models while improving reproducibility and transparency. Their framework standardizes interactions between models and benchmarks, supports scalable parallel evaluation, exposes hidden evaluation assumptions, and provides a consolidated leaderboard. All artifacts (framework, configs, and reproduction runs) are released to promote standardized, open VLA evaluation.

title: Creative Convergence or Imitation? Genre-Specific Homogeneity in LLM-Generated Chinese Literature
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in narrative generation. However, they often produce structurally homogenized stories, frequently following repetitive arrangements and combinations of plot events along with stereotypical resolutions. In this paper, we propose a novel theoretical framework for analysis by incorporating Proppian narratology and narrative functions. This framework is used to analyze the composition of narrative texts generated by LLMs to uncover their underlying narrative logic. Taking Chinese web literature as our research focus, we extend Propp's narrative theory, defining 34 narrative functions suited to modern web narrative structures. We further construct a human-annotated corpus to support the analysis of narrative structures within LLM-generated text. Experiments reveal that the primary reasons for the singular narrative logic and severe homogenization in generated texts are that current LLMs are unable to correctly comprehend the meanings of narrative functions and instead adhere to rigid narrative generation paradigms.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why LLM-generated stories are structurally homogeneous using a Propp-based narratology framework adapted to Chinese web fiction, and attributes the issue to LLMs’ shallow handling of narrative functions.
AI.motivation: LLMs can generate fluent narratives but their plots are repetitive, formulaic, and end in stereotypical ways. Existing evaluations focus on surface quality (fluency, coherence) rather than deeper narrative structure. There is a need for a systematic, theory-grounded way to explain and diagnose the structural homogeneity of LLM-generated stories, especially in the context of highly patterned genres like Chinese web literature.
AI.method: The authors build a theoretical analysis framework that combines Propp’s narratology with narrative function analysis, extending Propp’s original set of functions to 34 functions tailored to modern Chinese web fiction. They then construct a human-annotated corpus of LLM-generated narratives labeled with these functions and use this corpus to analyze the frequency, ordering, and combination patterns of narrative functions in LLM outputs.
AI.result: The analysis shows that LLM outputs exhibit a narrow range of function combinations and rigid, repetitive patterns of plot development and resolution. LLMs fail to appropriately interpret or deploy diverse narrative functions, leading to overuse of certain functions and underuse or misplacement of others, which explains the homogenized narrative structures observed in practice.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs rely on rigid narrative generation paradigms rather than a genuine understanding of narrative functions, which leads to singular narrative logic and severe structural homogenization in generated stories. The proposed Propp-based framework and annotated corpus provide tools to diagnose this problem and offer a foundation for designing future models or training strategies that better capture diverse narrative functions and richer plot structures.

title: Supervised Fine-Tuning versus Reinforcement Learning: A Study of Post-Training Methods for Large Language Models
summary: Pre-trained Large Language Model (LLM) exhibits broad capabilities, yet, for specific tasks or domains their attainment of higher accuracy and more reliable reasoning generally depends on post-training through Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) or Reinforcement Learning (RL). Although often treated as distinct methodologies, recent theoretical and empirical developments demonstrate that SFT and RL are closely connected. This study presents a comprehensive and unified perspective on LLM post-training with SFT and RL. We first provide an in-depth overview of both techniques, examining their objectives, algorithmic structures, and data requirements. We then systematically analyze their interplay, highlighting frameworks that integrate SFT and RL, hybrid training pipelines, and methods that leverage their complementary strengths. Drawing on a representative set of recent application studies from 2023 to 2025, we identify emerging trends, characterize the rapid shift toward hybrid post-training paradigms, and distill key takeaways that clarify when and why each method is most effective. By synthesizing theoretical insights, practical methodologies, and empirical evidence, this study establishes a coherent understanding of SFT and RL within a unified framework and outlines promising directions for future research in scalable, efficient, and generalizable LLM post-training.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a unified survey and framework connecting Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) for post-training large language models, emphasizing their relationships, hybrid approaches, and future directions.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are powerful, achieving high accuracy and reliable reasoning on specific tasks still requires post-training. SFT and RL are usually viewed as separate, but recent work shows they are closely related. There is a need for a comprehensive, unified perspective that explains their connections, trade-offs, and when to use each, to guide research and practice.
AI.method: The authors conduct a structured, theory-informed survey and synthesis. They review the objectives, algorithms, and data needs of SFT and RL; analyze theoretical links between them; categorize hybrid and integrated SFT+RL pipelines; and summarize findings from a curated set of application papers from 2023–2025. They then organize these elements into a unified conceptual framework for LLM post-training.
AI.result: The paper systematizes how SFT and RL relate, which hybrid strategies are emerging, and how they are being applied in recent LLM work. It identifies common design patterns, data and optimization trade-offs, and usage scenarios where each method (or their combination) is most effective. It also documents a clear trend toward hybrid post-training paradigms in the recent literature.
AI.conclusion: SFT and RL should be viewed as complementary components of a unified LLM post-training toolbox rather than isolated methods. Understanding their connections enables more scalable, efficient, and robust training pipelines. The paper’s unified framework and summarized evidence provide guidance on method selection and combination, and it outlines open challenges and promising directions for future research on generalizable LLM post-training.

title: Echoes Across Centuries: Phonetic Signatures of Persian Poets
summary:
This study examines phonetic texture in Persian poetry as a literary-historical phenomenon rather than a by-product of meter or a feature used only for classification. The analysis draws on a large corpus of 1,116,306 mesras from 31,988 poems written by 83 poets, restricted to five major classical meters to enable controlled comparison. Each line is converted into a grapheme-to-phoneme representation and analyzed using six phonetic metrics: hardness, sonority, sibilance, vowel ratio, phoneme entropy, and consonant-cluster ratio.
  Statistical models estimate poet-level differences while controlling for meter, poetic form, and line length. The results show that although meter and form explain a substantial portion of phonetic variation, they do not eliminate systematic differences between poets. Persian poetic sound therefore appears as conditioned variation within shared prosodic structures rather than as either purely individual style or simple metrical residue.
  A multidimensional stylistic map reveals several recurrent phonetic profiles, including high-sonority lyric styles, hardness-driven rhetorical or epic styles, sibilant mystical contours, and high-entropy complex textures. Historical analysis indicates that phonetic distributions shift across centuries, reflecting changes in genre prominence, literary institutions, and performance contexts rather than abrupt stylistic breaks.
  The study establishes a corpus-scale framework for phonetic analysis in Persian poetry and demonstrates how computational phonetics can contribute to literary-historical interpretation while remaining attentive to the formal structures that shape Persian verse.

AI.tldr: Large-scale computational analysis of phonetic texture in Persian poetry shows systematic poet- and period-level sound patterns beyond what is dictated by meter and form.
AI.motivation: To move beyond treating sound patterning in Persian poetry as either a mechanical effect of meter or a marginal stylistic feature, and instead understand it as a central, historically evolving dimension of poetic style that can be quantified at corpus scale.
AI.method: Construct a corpus of over one million half-lines (mesras) from nearly 32,000 poems by 83 poets, restricted to five common classical meters for comparability. Convert each line to a grapheme-to-phoneme representation, compute six quantitative phonetic metrics (hardness, sonority, sibilance, vowel ratio, phoneme entropy, consonant-cluster ratio), and fit statistical models that estimate poet-level phonetic tendencies while controlling for meter, poetic form, and line length. Use multidimensional analysis to map stylistic space and examine historical shifts in phonetic distributions.
AI.result: Meter and poetic form account for a large share of phonetic variation, but meaningful, systematic differences between poets persist even after controlling for these factors. The multidimensional map reveals recurring clusters of phonetic styles—e.g., high-sonority lyric, hardness-driven rhetorical/epic, sibilant mystical, and high-entropy complex textures.
