RAPO’s reaction-aware, hybrid scalar-verbal optimization framework yields denser, more informative training signals, enabling policies that better track and influence users’ emotional trajectories. The work suggests that optimizing over interaction consequences via natural-language feedback is a promising direction for aligning conversational agents with real-world emotional support goals.

title: Unlocking the Value of Text: Event-Driven Reasoning and Multi-Level Alignment for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Existing time series forecasting methods primarily rely on the numerical data itself. However, real-world time series exhibit complex patterns associated with multimodal information, making them difficult to predict with numerical data alone. While several multimodal time series forecasting methods have emerged, they either utilize text with limited supplementary information or focus merely on representation extraction, extracting minimal textual information for forecasting. To unlock the Value of Text, we propose VoT, a method with Event-driven Reasoning and Multi-level Alignment. Event-driven Reasoning combines the rich information in exogenous text with the powerful reasoning capabilities of LLMs for time series forecasting. To guide the LLMs in effective reasoning, we propose the Historical In-context Learning that retrieves and applies historical examples as in-context guidance. To maximize the utilization of text, we propose Multi-level Alignment. At the representation level, we utilize the Endogenous Text Alignment to integrate the endogenous text information with the time series. At the prediction level, we design the Adaptive Frequency Fusion to fuse the frequency components of event-driven prediction and numerical prediction to achieve complementary advantages. Experiments on real-world datasets across 10 domains demonstrate significant improvements over existing methods, validating the effectiveness of our approach in the utilization of text. The code is made available at https://github.com/decisionintelligence/VoT.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes VoT, a multimodal time series forecasting method that fully exploits textual information using event-driven reasoning with LLMs and multi-level alignment between text and numerical data, achieving significant performance gains on diverse real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Traditional time series forecasting methods depend mainly on numerical data, overlooking rich multimodal (especially textual) context such as events and exogenous information. Existing multimodal methods often use text only superficially or focus on representation extraction without deeply leveraging textual semantics for forecasting. The authors aim to better exploit the "Value of Text" to improve forecasting accuracy in complex real-world scenarios.
AI.method: The authors introduce VoT, which has two main components: (1) Event-driven Reasoning, where exogenous textual information about events is combined with large language models (LLMs). A Historical In-context Learning mechanism retrieves relevant historical examples and feeds them as in-context prompts to guide the LLM’s reasoning about future time series behavior. (2) Multi-level Alignment, which aligns text and numerical data at both representation and prediction levels. At the representation level, Endogenous Text Alignment integrates endogenous text (textual descriptions directly related to the target series) with the numerical sequence. At the prediction level, Adaptive Frequency Fusion fuses the frequency-domain components of the event-driven (text-based) prediction and the purely numerical prediction to exploit their complementary strengths.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world datasets from 10 different domains show that VoT significantly outperforms existing unimodal and multimodal forecasting baselines. The results indicate that combining LLM-based event reasoning with careful alignment and fusion of text and numerical time series yields marked accuracy improvements.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that textual information, when properly modeled through event-driven reasoning with LLMs and aligned with numerical data at multiple levels, can substantially enhance time series forecasting. VoT effectively unlocks the "Value of Text" and demonstrates a general, domain-agnostic framework for multimodal forecasting, as validated by strong empirical gains across various real-world datasets.

title: Agent Lifecycle Toolkit (ALTK): Reusable Middleware Components for Robust AI Agents
summary:
As AI agents move from demos into enterprise deployments, their failure modes become consequential: a misinterpreted tool argument can corrupt production data, a silent reasoning error can go undetected until damage is done, and outputs that violate organizational policy can create legal or compliance risk. Yet, most agent frameworks leave builders to handle these failure modes ad hoc, resulting in brittle, one-off safeguards that are hard to reuse or maintain. We present the Agent Lifecycle Toolkit (ALTK), an open-source collection of modular middleware components that systematically address these gaps across the full agent lifecycle.
  Across the agent lifecycle, we identify opportunities to intervene and improve, namely, post-user-request, pre-LLM prompt conditioning, post-LLM output processing, pre-tool validation, post-tool result checking, and pre-response assembly. ALTK provides modular middleware that detects, repairs, and mitigates common failure modes. It offers consistent interfaces that fit naturally into existing pipelines. It is compatible with low-code and no-code tools such as the ContextForge MCP Gateway and Langflow. Finally, it significantly reduces the effort of building reliable, production-grade agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Agent Lifecycle Toolkit (ALTK), a modular middleware framework for making AI agents safer and more reliable in enterprise deployments.
AI.motivation: AI agents in real-world enterprise settings can fail in harmful ways—corrupting data, silently reasoning incorrectly, or violating policies—while current frameworks offer only ad hoc, hard-to-maintain safeguards.
AI.method: The authors design and implement ALTK, an open-source set of modular middleware components that plug into multiple stages of the agent lifecycle (after user requests, before LLM prompting, after LLM outputs, before tool calls, after tool results, and before final responses) to detect, repair, and mitigate common failure modes with standardized interfaces.
AI.result: ALTK integrates with existing agent pipelines and low/no-code tools like ContextForge MCP Gateway and Langflow, providing a consistent middleware layer that reduces engineering effort and improves reliability and maintainability of production agents.
AI.conclusion: Systematically inserting modular, reusable middleware at key lifecycle stages enables safer and more reliable deployment of AI agents, making it easier to build and operate production-grade systems than with bespoke, ad hoc safeguards.

title: Talk, Evaluate, Diagnose: User-aware Agent Evaluation with Automated Error Analysis
comment: Accepted as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. Code and dataset are available in the repository https://github.com/SAP-samples/agent-quality-inspect
summary: Agent applications are increasingly adopted to automate workflows across diverse tasks. However, due to the heterogeneous domains they operate in, it is challenging to create a scalable evaluation framework. Prior works each employ their own methods to determine task success, such as database lookups, regex match, etc., adding complexity to the development of a unified agent evaluation approach. Moreover, they do not systematically account for the user's role nor expertise in the interaction, providing incomplete insights into the agent's performance. We argue that effective agent evaluation goes beyond correctness alone, incorporating conversation quality, efficiency and systematic diagnosis of agent errors. To address this, we introduce the TED framework (Talk, Evaluate, Diagnose). (1) Talk: We leverage reusable, generic expert and non-expert user persona templates for user-agent interaction. (2) Evaluate: We adapt existing datasets by representing subgoals-such as tool signatures, and responses-as natural language grading notes, evaluated automatically with LLM-as-a-judge. We propose new metrics that capture both turn efficiency and intermediate progress of the agent complementing the user-aware setup. (3) Diagnose: We introduce an automated error analysis tool that analyzes the inconsistencies of the judge and agents, uncovering common errors, and providing actionable feedback for agent improvement. We show that our TED framework reveals new insights regarding agent performance across models and user expertise levels. We also demonstrate potential gains in agent performance with peaks of 8-10% on our proposed metrics after incorporating the identified error remedies into the agent's design.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TED, a unified framework to evaluate and diagnose multi-domain agent applications, capturing not only correctness but also conversation quality, efficiency, and systematic error patterns.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of agentic workflows are fragmented, domain-specific, and rely on ad-hoc success criteria (e.g., regex, DB checks), making them hard to scale and compare. They also overlook how different user roles and expertise levels affect performance, and they largely reduce evaluation to correctness, missing aspects like interaction quality, efficiency, and structured error understanding. A more holistic, reusable, and user-aware evaluation framework is needed to guide agent design and improvement.
AI.method: The authors design TED (Talk, Evaluate, Diagnose): (1) Talk: create reusable expert and non-expert user persona templates that interact with agents in a standardized way. (2) Evaluate: convert task subgoals (e.g., correct tool signatures, intermediate responses) into natural language grading notes, then automatically assess them using an LLM-as-a-judge. They define new metrics that measure turn efficiency and intermediate progress conditioned on user expertise. (3) Diagnose: build an automated error analysis tool that compares agent behavior with judge assessments, detects inconsistencies, clusters common error types, and produces actionable feedback for improving the agent’s design.
AI.result: Using TED, the authors uncover new insights about how different models perform under different user expertise levels and interaction conditions. The new metrics reveal aspects of performance not visible under correctness-only evaluation. By applying the diagnoses to refine agent design, they achieve performance gains of roughly 8–10% on their proposed metrics, indicating that TED’s feedback is practically useful for improving agents.
AI.conclusion: TED provides a scalable, user-aware, and multifaceted framework for evaluating agentic systems, extending beyond binary correctness to also measure interaction quality, efficiency, and error patterns. Its automated evaluation and diagnosis components offer reusable infrastructure that exposes nuanced performance differences across models and users, and its feedback can lead to tangible improvements in agent behavior.

title: Understanding Reasoning in LLMs through Strategic Information Allocation under Uncertainty
summary: LLMs often exhibit Aha moments during reasoning, such as apparent self-correction following tokens like "Wait," yet their underlying mechanisms remain unclear. We introduce an information-theoretic framework that decomposes reasoning into procedural information and epistemic verbalization - the explicit externalization of uncertainty that supports downstream control actions. We show that purely procedural reasoning can become informationally stagnant, whereas epistemic verbalization enables continued information acquisition and is critical for achieving information sufficiency. Empirical results demonstrate that strong reasoning performance is driven by uncertainty externalization rather than specific surface tokens. Our framework unifies prior findings on Aha moments and post-training experiments, and offers insights for future reasoning model design.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an information-theoretic view of LLM "Aha moments," distinguishing between hidden procedural reasoning and explicit epistemic verbalization of uncertainty, and shows that externalizing uncertainty is key to strong reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs often appear to correct themselves mid-generation (e.g., after saying "Wait,"), but it is unclear what mechanisms drive this and how to formalize such reasoning behavior. Understanding this can guide the design of better reasoning models.
AI.method: The authors build an information-theoretic framework that separates reasoning into (1) procedural information (internal steps) and (2) epistemic verbalization (explicit expression of uncertainty). They analyze how each contributes to information gain and sufficiency, and perform empirical experiments testing whether performance gains stem from specific tokens like "Wait" or from broader patterns of uncertainty externalization.
AI.result: They find that purely procedural reasoning can plateau in terms of information gain, becoming informationally stagnant, while epistemic verbalization allows models to continue acquiring useful information. Empirically, they show that strong reasoning performance correlates with patterns of uncertainty externalization rather than reliance on particular trigger tokens such as "Wait."
AI.conclusion: Externalizing uncertainty is central to effective LLM reasoning and "Aha moments." The proposed framework reconciles previous empirical observations about self-correction and post-training interventions, and suggests that future reasoning models should explicitly support and leverage epistemic verbalization to maintain information flow and improve problem solving.

title: Are Dilemmas and Conflicts in LLM Alignment Solvable? A View from Priority Graph
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) become more powerful and autonomous, they increasingly face conflicts and dilemmas in many scenarios. We first summarize and taxonomize these diverse conflicts. Then, we model the LLM's preferences to make different choices as a priority graph, where instructions and values are nodes, and the edges represent context-specific priorities determined by the model's output distribution. This graph reveals that a unified stable LLM alignment is very challenging, because the graph is neither static nor necessarily consistent in different contexts. Besides, it also reveals a potential vulnerability: priority hacking, where adversaries can craft deceptive contexts to manipulate the graph and bypass safety alignments. To counter this, we propose a runtime verification mechanism, enabling LLMs to query external sources to ground their context and resist manipulation. While this approach enhances robustness, we also acknowledge that many ethical and value dilemmas are philosophically irreducible, posing a long-term, open challenge for the future of AI alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes conflicts and dilemmas faced by powerful LLMs, models their context-dependent priorities as a graph, exposes a vulnerability called priority hacking, and proposes runtime verification via external sources as a partial mitigation, while noting that deep ethical dilemmas remain unresolved.
AI.motivation: As LLMs become more capable and autonomous, they encounter conflicting instructions, values, and objectives in real applications. Current alignment methods assume relatively stable preferences, but the authors are motivated to understand and formalize how LLMs actually prioritize between competing demands, and to identify emerging failure modes such as adversarial manipulation of these priorities (priority hacking).
AI.method: (1) Conceptually summarize and build a taxonomy of conflicts and dilemmas LLMs face. (2) Formalize the model’s conditional preferences as a priority graph whose nodes are instructions/values and whose directed edges represent context-specific priority relations inferred from the model’s output distribution. (3) Analyze properties of this graph across contexts to show instability and inconsistency, and identify an attack surface termed priority hacking where adversarial prompts reshape the priorities. (4) Propose a runtime verification framework where the LLM can query external tools or sources to ground and check its context, with the aim of resisting such manipulations, and discuss its implications and remaining challenges.
AI.result: The priority-graph analysis indicates that LLM alignment is not globally stable: priorities among instructions and values change with context and can be mutually inconsistent. This instability creates a concrete vulnerability—priority hacking—where carefully crafted contexts can cause safety-related priorities to be overridden. Preliminary exploration suggests that allowing the LLM to perform runtime verification via external sources can reduce susceptibility to such attacks by grounding decisions beyond the adversarially provided context.
AI.conclusion: Unified, stable alignment for powerful LLMs is intrinsically difficult because their context-dependent priority structures are dynamic and can be inconsistent, leaving them open to attacks such as priority hacking. Representing alignment as a priority graph clarifies this vulnerability and motivates defenses. Runtime verification, where models cross-check context through external queries, can improve robustness against manipulative prompts but cannot resolve fundamentally irreducible ethical and value conflicts, which remain a long-term open problem for AI alignment.

title: Computational Concept of the Psyche
summary: This article presents an overview of approaches to modeling the human psyche in the context of constructing an artificial one. Based on this overview, a concept of cognitive architecture is proposed, in which the psyche is viewed as the operating system of a living or artificial subject, comprising a space of states, including the state of needs that determine the meaning of a subject's being in relation to stimuli from the external world, and intelligence as a decision-making system regarding actions in this world to satisfy these needs. Based on this concept, a computational formalization is proposed for creating artificial general intelligence systems for an agent through experiential learning in a state space that includes agent's needs, taking into account their biological or existential significance for the intelligent agent, along with agent's sensations and actions. Thus, the problem of constructing artificial general intelligence is formalized as a system for making optimal decisions in the space of specific agent needs under conditions of uncertainty, maximizing success in achieving goals, minimizing existential risks, and maximizing energy efficiency. A minimal experimental implementation of the model is presented.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a cognitive architecture that treats the psyche as an operating system for an agent, integrating needs, perceptions, and actions into a state-space framework for artificial general intelligence (AGI), and demonstrates a minimal experimental implementation.
AI.motivation: To advance artificial general intelligence by grounding it in a computational model of the human psyche, where an agent’s internal needs and their significance drive perception, decision-making, and action, enabling more general, autonomous, and goal-directed behavior under uncertainty.
AI.method: The authors conceptually define the psyche as an operating system composed of a state space that includes needs, perceptions, and actions, and they formalize AGI as an optimal decision-making problem in this augmented state space. They then implement a minimal experimental prototype of this architecture, where an agent learns from experience to select actions that satisfy its modeled needs while accounting for existential risk and energy efficiency.
AI.result: The work yields a formal computational model that embeds needs and their biological or existential significance into an agent’s state representation and decision-making process, along with an initial minimal implementation demonstrating the feasibility of experiential learning in such a need-centered state space.
AI.conclusion: Modeling the psyche as an operating system with explicit representation of needs allows AGI to be framed as optimal decision-making under uncertainty in a needs-based state space. This perspective offers a principled way to design agents that maximize goal achievement, minimize existential risks, and optimize energy use, with preliminary experiments indicating the practical viability of the approach.

title: OpenSeeker: Democratizing Frontier Search Agents by Fully Open-Sourcing Training Data
summary: Deep search capabilities have become an indispensable competency for frontier Large Language Model (LLM) agents, yet the development of high-performance search agents remains dominated by industrial giants due to a lack of transparent, high-quality training data. This persistent data scarcity has fundamentally hindered the progress of the broader research community in developing and innovating within this domain. To bridge this gap, we introduce OpenSeeker, the first fully open-source search agent (i.e., model and data) that achieves frontier-level performance through two core technical innovations: (1) Fact-grounded scalable controllable QA synthesis, which reverse-engineers the web graph via topological expansion and entity obfuscation to generate complex, multi-hop reasoning tasks with controllable coverage and complexity. (2) Denoised trajectory synthesis, which employs a retrospective summarization mechanism to denoise the trajectory, therefore promoting the teacher LLMs to generate high-quality actions. Experimental results demonstrate that OpenSeeker, trained (a single training run) on only 11.7k synthesized samples, achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks including BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, xbench-DeepSearch, and WideSearch. Notably, trained with simple SFT, OpenSeeker significantly outperforms the second-best fully open-source agent DeepDive (e.g., 29.5% v.s. 15.3% on BrowseComp), and even surpasses industrial competitors such as Tongyi DeepResearch (trained via extensive continual pre-training, SFT, and RL) on BrowseComp-ZH (48.4% v.s. 46.7%). We fully open-source the complete training dataset and the model weights to democratize frontier search agent research and foster a more transparent, collaborative ecosystem.

AI.tldr: OpenSeeker is a fully open-source large language model–based web search agent that reaches frontier-level performance using a small but carefully synthesized training set and is released with both data and weights to democratize research.
AI.motivation: High-performing LLM-based search agents require deep web search capabilities and high-quality training trajectories, but existing leading systems and their data are proprietary, leaving academia and smaller labs without transparent resources to build or study such agents. This data scarcity slows innovation and prevents reproducible research on advanced search agents.
AI.method: They build OpenSeeker with two key techniques: (1) Fact-grounded scalable controllable QA synthesis that constructs complex multi-hop web search tasks by reverse-engineering the web graph using topological expansion and entity obfuscation, letting them control task coverage and difficulty while grounding in real facts. (2) Denoised trajectory synthesis that retrospectively summarizes and cleans noisy search trajectories so that teacher LLMs can generate higher-quality action sequences for supervised fine-tuning. The model is then trained in a single SFT run on 11.7k such synthesized examples.
AI.result: With only 11.7k synthesized training samples and simple supervised fine-tuning, OpenSeeker reaches state-of-the-art performance on multiple deep search benchmarks: BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, xbench-DeepSearch, and WideSearch. It substantially outperforms the best fully open-source baseline DeepDive (e.g., 29.5% vs. 15.3% on BrowseComp) and even surpasses an industrial system, Tongyi DeepResearch, on BrowseComp-ZH (48.4% vs. 46.7%).
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed synthetic data generation—via fact-grounded, controllable QA construction and denoised trajectory synthesis—is sufficient for training an open-source LLM search agent that matches or exceeds proprietary frontier systems. By releasing both the model and the complete training dataset, OpenSeeker lowers the barrier to research on deep search agents and encourages a more transparent and collaborative ecosystem.

title: Do Metrics for Counterfactual Explanations Align with User Perception?
comment: Accepted at the 4th World Conference on eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI 2026)
summary: Explainability is widely regarded as essential for trustworthy artificial intelligence systems. However, the metrics commonly used to evaluate counterfactual explanations are algorithmic evaluation metrics that are rarely validated against human judgments of explanation quality. This raises the question of whether such metrics meaningfully reflect user perceptions. We address this question through an empirical study that directly compares algorithmic evaluation metrics with human judgments across three datasets. Participants rated counterfactual explanations along multiple dimensions of perceived quality, which we relate to a comprehensive set of standard counterfactual metrics. We analyze both individual relationships and the extent to which combinations of metrics can predict human assessments. Our results show that correlations between algorithmic metrics and human ratings are generally weak and strongly dataset-dependent. Moreover, increasing the number of metrics used in predictive models does not lead to reliable improvements, indicating structural limitations in how current metrics capture criteria relevant for humans. Overall, our findings suggest that widely used counterfactual evaluation metrics fail to reflect key aspects of explanation quality as perceived by users, underscoring the need for more human-centered approaches to evaluating explainable artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically tests whether standard algorithmic metrics for counterfactual explanations align with human judgments and finds they mostly do not.
AI.motivation: Explainable AI often relies on counterfactual explanations, whose quality is typically assessed with algorithmic metrics (e.g., proximity, sparsity). These metrics are widely used but rarely validated against what humans actually perceive as good explanations, creating a potential gap between technical evaluation and real-world usefulness.
AI.method: The authors conduct a user study across three datasets, collecting human ratings of counterfactual explanations along multiple perceived quality dimensions. They compute a broad suite of standard counterfactual evaluation metrics for the same explanations and analyze correlations between each metric and human ratings. They also build predictive models combining multiple metrics to see how well they jointly predict human assessments.
AI.result: They find that correlations between individual algorithmic metrics and human ratings are generally weak and vary substantially by dataset. Combining more metrics into predictive models does not consistently improve alignment with human judgments, suggesting that current metrics systematically miss important aspects of perceived explanation quality.
AI.conclusion: Current, widely used algorithmic metrics for evaluating counterfactual explanations do not reliably reflect human perceptions of explanation quality. This misalignment appears structural rather than just a matter of combining more metrics. The paper argues for developing more human-centered evaluation approaches for explainable AI, grounded in user studies and human criteria rather than purely technical proxies.
